title: A rigorous and robust quantum speed-up in supervised machine learning
authors: Yunchao Liu, Srinivasan Arunachalam, Kristan Temme
one_line: Constructs a discrete-logarithm-based classification task that is provably hard for any classical learner (no better than random guessing under the DLP hardness assumption) yet is efficiently and noise-robustly learnable by a support vector machine using a quantum-kernel feature map, giving the first end-to-end provable quantum speed-up in supervised learning with only classical data access.
methodology: The authors define a concept class C = {f_s} over Z_p* where f_s labels x by whether log_g(x) falls in a half-circle interval [s, s+(p-3)/2]; because computing the discrete log is classically hard, the labels look random to any efficient classical learner. They map each data point x to an n-qubit "interval state" |phi(x)> = (1/sqrt(2^k)) sum_i |x·g^i> (n = ceil(log2 p), k = n - t·log n), preparable in BQP via Shor's algorithm in O~(n^3) gates, and use Quantum Kernel Estimation (QKE) to estimate K(x_i,x_j) = |<phi(x_i)|phi(x_j)>|^2 as the 0^n measurement frequency of U†(x_i)U(x_j)|0^n>. A classical L2 soft-margin SVM dual program then finds a large-margin separator; a separating "halfspace state" |phi_s> exists with margin Delta = 2^{k+1}/p = O(n^{-t}). They prove noise robustness against finite-sampling additive kernel error (mean 0, variance 1/R) via strong-convexity perturbation analysis plus a strengthened fat-shattering generalization bound.
datasets: A purpose-engineered synthetic family of datasets parameterized by a prime p and generator g of Z_p*. Data space X = Z_p* (n = ceil(log2 p)-bit integers), uniform distribution; concept secret key s in Z_p*; binary labels {-1,+1} split 50/50. Training set size m = poly(n) (analysis uses m = n^c with t = c/3). No fixed numeric p, n, or m instantiated; no real-world data. Labeled examples can be generated classically in poly time despite labels being hard to compute (sample random y, label by interval, return (g^y, label)).
baselines: Classical baseline: ALL efficient (poly-time) classical learners, shown to be limited to random guessing (50% accuracy) under DLP hardness — this is a universal lower bound, not a single competing model. Quantum learner: SVM with quantum kernel estimation (SVM-QKE). Conceptual contrast is also drawn against (a) amplitude-encoding QML with quantum data access (vulnerable to dequantization per Tang et al.) and (b) heuristic variational/quantum-neural-network classifiers lacking provable guarantees.
metrics: Test accuracy = Pr_{x~X}[f*(x) = f(x)]; classification margin Delta; generalization/test-error bound; number of measurement shots R for noise variance 1/R; gate complexity O~(n^3); sample complexity m = poly(n).
key_results: Theorem 1/8: under classical hardness of DLP, no efficient classical algorithm can achieve test accuracy beyond 1/2 + 1/poly(n) (i.e., essentially random guessing) for C. Theorem 2/22: SVM-QKE provably achieves test accuracy >= 0.99 in polynomial time with success probability >= 2/3 over random samples and QKE noise, for every concept in C. Quantitative generalization bound: test error Pr[y·h'(x) < 0] <= O~(1/(m·Delta^2) + Delta), optimized to O~(m^{-1/3}) by choosing t = c/3 for m = n^c. Margin Delta = 2^{k+1}/p = O(n^{-t}). Kernel estimation itself is DLP-hard: Lemma 12 shows estimating K_0(y,y') to 0.01 additive error solves DLP. Feature states prepared with O~(n^3) gates.
qubits: n = ceil(log2 p) qubits, where p is a large prime (chosen large enough that DLP is classically intractable; in practice p would be hundreds of bits, so n is in the hundreds-to-thousands of qubits and far exceeds 20). No simulation or hardware experiment was run, so no concrete qubit count <=20 or <=30 is demonstrated. The speed-up regime requires fault-tolerant scale qubits (DLP-hardness needs n large); it is NOT instantiated at the 20-30 qubit simulable scale.
classical_struggle: Yes — this is the canonical example of a task where classical methods provably fail and a quantum method provably wins, but with critical caveats for the 20+-qubit-simulation hypothesis. Strength: the classical hardness is a PROVEN reduction (no efficient classical learner beats 1/2 + 1/poly(n)) conditional on the widely-believed but UNPROVEN hardness of discrete log; it is robust to dequantization because the kernel itself is DLP-hard to estimate (Lemma 12) and uses only classical data access (immune to the Tang-style quantum-inspired attacks that killed amplitude-encoding speed-ups). The quantum side is also proven (Theorem 22), including noise robustness to finite-shot sampling error. KEY CAVEAT for simulability: the separation only exists asymptotically and requires n = ceil(log2 p) qubits with p large enough that DLP is hard — i.e., hundreds of bits/qubits and fault-tolerant Shor execution. At <=30 (or any classically simulable) qubit count, the discrete log is trivially solvable classically, so there is NO advantage in the simulable regime; one cannot demonstrate this speed-up with a 20-30 qubit simulation. Additional caveats: the task is artificial/not practically motivated (authors explicitly say so), the feature map embeds Shor's algorithm, the conditional hardness assumption (DLP) could fail, and DLP being easy quantumly means the kernel method is not exploiting anything beyond Shor. No empirical experiment is reported.
code: none (purely theoretical paper; no repository, no simulation, no Qiskit/PennyLane/TFQ implementation reported)
relevance: High relevance as the foundational theory reference for "classical-hard / quantum-easy" supervised learning, but LOW relevance as direct support for the specific hypothesis of demonstrating advantage via simulation with 20-30 qubits. It rigorously establishes that such separating tasks EXIST (under DLP hardness) and that quantum kernel methods can be provably advantageous and noise-robust; however, the advantage is asymptotic and only manifests when the discrete log is classically intractable, which requires far more than 30 qubits and fault-tolerance, making the separation impossible to exhibit in a small classically-simulable regime. Essential to cite for framing, motivation, and the QKE methodology, but its construction cannot itself be the simulable demonstration.

#### liu2021_rigorous_quantum_speedup

**A rigorous and robust quantum speed-up in supervised machine learning** (Liu, Arunachalam, Temme; arXiv 2020 / Nature Physics 2021).

This paper provides the theoretical bedrock for the claim that there exist supervised-learning tasks where classical methods provably fail while quantum methods succeed, using only *classical* access to data. The authors engineer a binary-classification concept class C = {f_s} over Z_p* defined via the discrete logarithm: f_s(x) = +1 iff log_g(x) lies in the half-interval [s, s+(p-3)/2]. After taking the discrete log the problem is a trivial 1-D clustering task (Fig. 1a), but in the original data space the labels are indistinguishable from random to any efficient classical learner (Fig. 1b).

**Main results.** Under the widely-believed classical hardness of the discrete log problem (DLP), they prove (Thm 1/8) that *no* efficient classical algorithm can exceed 1/2 + 1/poly(n) test accuracy — i.e., it cannot beat random guessing. Conversely, they give a quantum support vector machine with Quantum Kernel Estimation (SVM-QKE) that provably attains >= 0.99 test accuracy in polynomial time with success probability >= 2/3 (Thm 2/22). The quantum feature map sends x to an n-qubit "interval state" |phi(x)> = (1/sqrt(2^k)) sum_i |x·g^i> (n = ceil(log2 p)), preparable in BQP with O~(n^3) gates using Shor's algorithm; the kernel K(x_i,x_j) = |<phi(x_i)|phi(x_j)>|^2 is read off as the 0^n measurement frequency of U†(x_i)U(x_j)|0^n>, and a classical L2 soft-margin SVM finds a large-margin separator. A separating halfspace state exists with margin Delta = O(n^{-t}), yielding a generalization bound of test error O~(1/(m·Delta^2) + Delta), optimized to O~(m^{-1/3}). Crucially, the result is *robust*: with finite-shot kernel noise (mean 0, variance 1/R), strong convexity plus a strengthened fat-shattering generalization bound preserve the guarantee.

**Robustness to dequantization.** Unlike earlier amplitude-encoding speed-ups (which Tang-style quantum-inspired classical algorithms dequantized), this construction uses only classical data access, and Lemma 12 shows that even estimating a single kernel entry to 0.01 additive error is itself DLP-hard — so the speed-up cannot be classically simulated.

**Critical assessment for the present hypothesis.** This is the strongest possible kind of "classical struggles, quantum wins" evidence: the classical lower bound is a *proof* (conditional on DLP hardness) covering all efficient classical learners, and the quantum upper bound is also proved end-to-end including noise. However, two caveats are decisive for a 20-30-qubit *simulable* demonstration. First, the separation is asymptotic and exists *only* in the regime where DLP is classically intractable, which requires p of hundreds of bits and therefore n = ceil(log2 p) qubits in the hundreds-to-thousands plus fault-tolerant Shor execution. At any classically simulable scale (<=30 qubits) the discrete log is trivially solvable, so there is no advantage to exhibit. Second, the task is admittedly artificial ("not practically motivated") and the feature map essentially embeds Shor's algorithm; the underlying DLP assumption, while standard, is unproven. There is no code, no simulation, and no empirical experiment. **Verdict:** indispensable as the conceptual and methodological anchor (QKE, provable separation, noise robustness), but it cannot itself serve as a simulable 20-30 qubit demonstration of advantage.

title: Power of data in quantum machine learning
authors: Hsin-Yuan Huang, Michael Broughton, Masoud Mohseni, Ryan Babbush, Sergio Boixo, Hartmut Neven, Jarrod R. McClean (Google Research / Caltech)
one_line: Introduces a geometric-difference test (g) and rigorous prediction-error bounds showing that data can let classical ML match quantum models even on classically-hard-to-compute data, and proposes the projected quantum kernel which empirically beats all tested classical models on engineered datasets up to 30 qubits and gives a provable fault-tolerant speedup on a discrete-log task.
methodology: The authors derive a concentration-based prediction-error bound Ex|h(x)-f(x)| <= c*sqrt(s_K(N)/N) for kernel ML models learning a quantum function f(x)=Tr(O_U rho(x)), where the model complexity s_K and an asymmetric geometric difference g(K1||K2)=||sqrt(K2)K1^{-1}sqrt(K2)||_inf^{1/2} (computed via SVD of N x N kernel matrices) act as a function-independent pre-screen for potential quantum advantage (s_C <= g_CQ^2 s_Q). Because the standard quantum (fidelity) kernel Tr(rho(xi)rho(xj)) yields tiny inner products in exponentially large Hilbert space (g small, dimension d ~ N), they introduce projected quantum kernels that project states back to a low-dimensional classical representation (1-RDMs / classical shadows), e.g. k_PQ = exp(-gamma * sum_k ||rho_k(xi)-rho_k(xj)||_F^2), enlarging g. They then construct, via a generalized eigenvalue problem, engineered label functions that saturate s_C = g^2 s_Q to maximize the quantum/classical prediction gap. Simulations use TensorFlow-Quantum on PCA-reduced fashion-MNIST embedded with three circuits (E1 separable rotations, E2 IQP-style, E3 Heisenberg Trotter evolution).
datasets: Fashion-MNIST images (shirt vs dress) reduced via PCA to n-dimensional vectors (n = number of qubits), normalized, subsampled to 800 points; N=600 (and N=100/600) used for evaluation. Quantum labels are expectation values of a local observable after a random-coupling 1D-Heisenberg Trotter QNN. Engineered/adversarial datasets are synthesized by solving a generalized eigenvalue problem to saturate the geometric inequality s_C = g(K_C||K_PQ)^2; converted to a binary classification task via median thresholding. A separate synthetic discrete-logarithm dataset (Definition 1) is used for the provable speedup.
baselines: Quantum: original quantum (fidelity) kernel Tr(rho rho) and the projected quantum kernel. Classical: best-performing among Gaussian SVM, linear SVM, AdaBoost, random forest, neural networks, and gradient boosting (with tuned hyperparameters); linear and Gaussian classical kernels (with a sweep over lambda and gamma) used to minimize g and find the strongest classical adversary; neural tangent kernels referenced as an option.
metrics: Prediction error (regression, lower better) and classification accuracy (higher better); approximate effective dimension d = rank(K_Q); geometric difference g; model complexity s_K(N); VC-dimension and sample complexity for the discrete-log proof.
key_results: On engineered datasets the projected quantum kernel outperforms the best classical model with an accuracy advantage exceeding 20% at large system sizes (up to 30 qubits), and the advantage tracks g (small for E1, moderate E2, large E3); the original quantum kernel and classical methods both decline precipitously as g grows. On natural fashion-MNIST quantum-labeled tasks the original quantum kernel shows no advantage (g small, d grows ~N) and classical ML is competitive or better; only PQ with E3 shows mild advantage. Theoretical: number of samples needed scales as min(d, Tr(O^2)); quantum kernel matches a proven lower bound N >= Omega(Tr(O^2)/eps^2) (mutual-information argument). Projected quantum kernel solves the discrete-log learning problem with sample complexity N = O(log(1/eps)/eps^2), independent of input size n (VC-dimension 3), giving a provable advantage. Quantum-kernel limitation: a trivial linear function (O = Z, Tr(O^2)=2^n) requires N >= (1-eps)2^n samples for the quantum kernel but only O(n) classically. Largest gate-based QML simulation to date: ~1.1 petaflop/s peak, ~2 exaflop total.
qubits: Simulation only (no hardware); engineered-dataset experiments scale up to 30 qubits (system size n in plots runs to ~30); original quantum kernel reported only up to n=28 due to simulation cost and poor performance. Well above the 20-qubit threshold of interest.
classical_struggle: Yes, but with important nuance. (1) Empirical: on engineered datasets the projected quantum kernel beats ALL tested classical models (including kernel-free ones like random forests) by >20% accuracy at up to 30 simulated qubits when g is large (E3 embedding) -- the first empirical demonstration of such a large quantum/classical ML prediction separation, robust to moderate noise and stable across classical methods. Caveat: this is a deliberately adversarially-engineered label function chosen to saturate s_C=g^2 s_Q, not a naturally occurring task; the advantage shrinks as N grows ("power of data"), and g is minimized only over a finite classical-model suite, so a stronger/unconsidered classical model could narrow the gap. (2) Provable (not just empirical): the discrete-log projected-kernel result is a rigorous separation, but it relies on Shor's algorithm for the feature map and the hardness of discrete log -- i.e. it lives in the fault-tolerant regime and is not a 30-qubit-simulable advantage. (3) The paper itself emphasizes the negative direction: data elevates classical ML so that naive quantum kernels on classically-hard data usually show NO advantage (small g), and warns that claims of advantage require benchmarking against classical approximations, not just classical ML. So the genuine simulable (<=30 qubit) win is empirical and on engineered data; the only fully rigorous win needs fault-tolerance.
code: Built on TensorFlow and TensorFlow-Quantum (Broughton et al. 2020) with scikit-learn for PCA/SVM and Neural Tangents referenced; no explicit public repository link given in the paper text.
relevance: high -- Directly central to the hypothesis: it is the canonical paper formalizing WHEN classical ML struggles and quantum wins, provides the geometric-difference diagnostic, demonstrates an empirical quantum-over-classical ML separation via simulation at exactly the 20-30 qubit scale of interest, and supplies a concrete recipe (projected quantum kernel + engineered datasets) for constructing such tasks, while also delivering the key cautionary caveats (data dequantizes naive quantum advantage; rigorous separation needs fault tolerance).

#### huang2021_power_of_data

**Huang, Broughton, Mohseni, Babbush, Boixo, Neven, and McClean (2021), "Power of data in quantum machine learning."**

This Google/Caltech paper is the foundational reference for reasoning about *when* a quantum ML model can outperform a classical one given training data, and it is unusually balanced: it shows both that data often *erases* naive quantum advantage and how to *engineer* a genuine separation.

**Core idea.** For a supervised task whose labels are generated by a quantum function f(x) = Tr(O_U rho(x)), the authors prove a concentration-based prediction-error bound E|h(x)-f(x)| <= c*sqrt(s_K(N)/N), where the model complexity s_K and an asymmetric *geometric difference* g(K1||K2) = ||sqrt(K2) K1^{-1} sqrt(K2)||_inf^{1/2} (computed by SVD of N x N kernel matrices) determine the achievable gap via s_C <= g_CQ^2 s_Q. Small g guarantees a classical model matches the quantum model *for any labels*; large g certifies the *existence* of a dataset with a quantum prediction advantage, which can be constructed explicitly by a generalized eigenvalue problem saturating s_C = g^2 s_Q. A complexity-theoretic argument (BPP ⊆ BPP/samp ⊆ P/poly) makes rigorous the claim that data can let classical algorithms solve problems outside BPP.

**Projected quantum kernel.** The standard fidelity kernel Tr(rho(xi)rho(xj)) fails in the exponentially large Hilbert space: all states become nearly orthogonal (effective dimension d ~ N, g ~ 1), so classical ML matches or beats it. The authors instead *project* states back to a low-dimensional classical representation (1-RDMs / classical shadows), e.g. k_PQ = exp(-gamma * sum_k ||rho_k(xi) - rho_k(xj)||_F^2). This enlarges g while remaining hard to evaluate without a quantum computer.

**Evidence.** Using TensorFlow-Quantum on PCA-reduced fashion-MNIST with three embeddings (E1 separable, E2 IQP-style, E3 Heisenberg-Trotter), they run what was then the largest gate-based QML simulation (~1.1 petaflop/s peak, ~2 exaflop total). On natural quantum-labeled data, no meaningful advantage appears (g small); on *engineered* datasets that saturate g, the projected quantum kernel beats **every** tested classical model — Gaussian/linear SVM, AdaBoost, random forest, neural nets, gradient boosting — by **>20% accuracy at up to 30 qubits**, with the gap tracking g and remaining stable across kernel-free classical methods. They prove a matching lower bound N >= Omega(Tr(O^2)/eps^2) for the quantum kernel, and exhibit a trivial linear function requiring N >= (1-eps)2^n samples for the quantum kernel versus O(n) classically — motivating the projected variant.

**Relevance to the hypothesis (high).** This is the clearest existing demonstration of a *simulable* (≤30 qubit) empirical separation where classical ML struggles and a quantum method wins, plus a diagnostic (g) for finding such tasks. **Caveats are essential:** the simulable win is on *adversarially engineered* labels (not a natural task), g is minimized only over a finite classical-model suite, and the advantage diminishes as N grows. The only *rigorous* separation is the discrete-logarithm projected-kernel result (sample complexity O(log(1/eps)/eps^2), independent of n, via VC-dimension 3), which relies on Shor's algorithm and lives in the fault-tolerant regime rather than the 30-qubit simulable one. The paper explicitly cautions that true QML advantage claims must benchmark against classical *approximations* of quantum models, not just classical ML.

title: Supervised learning with quantum enhanced feature spaces
authors: Vojtech Havlicek, Antonio D. Corcoles, Kristan Temme, Aram W. Harrow, Abhinav Kandala, Jerry M. Chow, Jay M. Gambetta (IBM T.J. Watson Research Center; MIT)
one_line: Introduces two SVM-style classifiers that encode classical data into a quantum Hilbert space via a conjecturally-hard-to-simulate feature map—a variational quantum classifier and a quantum kernel estimator—and demonstrates them on a 2-qubit superconducting processor with up to 100% classification accuracy.
methodology: Classical data x is mapped non-linearly into an n-qubit quantum state via a feature map U_Phi(x) = U_Phi H^n U_Phi H^n, where U_Phi is a diagonal phase gate with single- and two-qubit (|S|<=2, Ising-type ZZ) interactions conjectured hard to simulate classically. Method 1 (variational classifier) applies a parameterized short-depth circuit W(theta) trained via Spall's SPSA to build a separating hyperplane in quantum state space. Method 2 (quantum kernel estimator) uses the quantum computer only to estimate the fidelity kernel K(x,z)=|<Phi(x)|Phi(z)>|^2 via the all-zeros transition amplitude of U_Phi(z)^dag U_Phi(x), then feeds K into a conventional classical SVM dual program. First-order zero-noise (Richardson) extrapolation error mitigation is applied throughout.
datasets: Artificially engineered 2-D synthetic data on Omega=(0,2pi]^2, constructed to be perfectly separable by the chosen feature map. Labels generated via a random V in SU(4) and parity f=Z1Z2: m=+1 if <Phi|V^dag f V|Phi> >= Delta, m=-1 if <= -Delta, with separation gap Delta=0.3. Training and test sets each have 20 points per label (40 total). Three datasets (Set I, II, III) were studied; no real-world data used.
baselines: Conceptual baseline is the conventional classical non-linear SVM (e.g., polynomial and infinite-dimensional Gaussian kernels). The two quantum methods are compared against each other (variational classifier vs. quantum kernel estimator). Product-state (no-entanglement) feature maps and single-layer/IQP-style feature maps are discussed as cases that are classically simulable and thus provide no advantage. No quantitative head-to-head against a trained classical SVM on this data is reported (the data is designed to be 100% separable by the quantum map).
metrics: Classification success rate (fraction of correctly labeled test points); empirical risk R_emp (expected misclassification probability) for training convergence; kernel matrix deviation ||K - K_hat|| (Frobenius/operator norm); inner product between experimental and ideal SVM hyperplanes; combined negative-eigenvalue weight of estimated kernels.
key_results: Variational classifier: success increases with circuit depth, reaching ~100% for depths l>1 and holding up to l=4 (8 CNOTs) despite decoherence; R_emp converges lower for l=4 than l=0. Quantum kernel estimator: Set I and Set II achieve 100% success over 10 random test sets each; Set III achieves 94.75% (attributed to slightly worse readout fidelity ~94%, and largest negative-eigenvalue kernel weight 2.41 vs 1.40/1.27 for Sets I/II). Training used 2000 shots (cost evaluated at R=200), classification at 10000 shots; kernel entries used 50000 shots each. Hardware: CNOT error 0.0373; single-qubit gate errors ~7e-4 to 1.4e-3; readout fidelity ~95%; T1~{55,38}us.
qubits: Experiment: 2 qubits (Q0, Q1) on a 5-qubit superconducting transmon device; only n=d=2 used. Theory/algorithms stated for general n qubits with feature space dimension 4^n, but no simulation or hardware runs at >=20 qubits are reported. All concrete results are at n=2.
classical_struggle: Only partially and conjecturally. The paper does NOT empirically demonstrate a task where a classical method fails and quantum wins: the synthetic data is explicitly engineered to be 100% separable by the chosen quantum feature map, and no classical baseline is shown to fail on it (verification of hardware, not advantage, is the stated goal). The claimed route to advantage rests entirely on a CONJECTURE that the kernel K=|<Phi(x)|Phi(z)>|^2 of the two-basis-change feature map is hard to estimate classically up to additive polynomial error, motivated by an analogy to the hidden-shift / bent-function problem (Roetteler) with a query-complexity (oracle) separation—not a proven separation for this kernel. The authors explicitly note caveats: (i) the single-layer/IQP version (Bremner et al.) is #P-hard only for multiplicative error, but its kernel is efficiently estimable classically to additive error via uniform sampling (R~eps^-2)—i.e., a dequantization for the simpler map; (ii) product-state and shallow maps are classically simulable; (iii) noisy quantum devices cannot give multiplicative-error estimates either. They state that if the conjecture could be proven it 'would establish a valuable step in rigorously establishing a quantum advantage,' and if it fails the circuit family must be made harder. Net: advantage is conjectured, not proven or empirically shown; and the regime studied (2 qubits) is trivially classically simulable.
code: None provided in the paper (no repository link). Methods later became QSVM / ZZFeatureMap in IBM Qiskit (qiskit-machine-learning), but no code is referenced in the manuscript.
relevance: High. This is a foundational, widely-cited core paper that directly frames the hypothesis: it proposes encoding classical data into a high-dimensional quantum feature/Hilbert space and argues a quantum advantage can arise specifically when the kernel is classically hard to estimate. It clearly delineates WHEN classical methods would and would not struggle (product/shallow maps = no advantage; conjecturally-hard entangling maps = potential advantage). Caveats for the hypothesis: the advantage is only conjectured (with an explicit dequantization of the simpler IQP variant), the demonstration uses only 2 qubits (not the >=20-qubit simulable regime targeted), and the data is engineered rather than a natural task where classical ML provably fails.

#### havlicek2019_quantum_feature_spaces

**Havlicek et al. (2019), "Supervised learning with quantum enhanced feature spaces"** (Nature 567:209-212; arXiv:1804.11326). This is a foundational paper for quantum kernel methods. The authors propose representing the feature space of a classification problem directly by a quantum state, mapping classical data x non-linearly into an n-qubit Hilbert space (dimension 4^n) via a feature map U_Phi(x) = U_Phi H^n U_Phi H^n, where U_Phi is a diagonal phase circuit built from single-qubit Z rotations and two-qubit ZZ (Ising) interactions (weight |S|<=2). They introduce two classifiers: (1) a **quantum variational classifier**, which applies a trainable short-depth circuit W(theta) (optimized with SPSA) to construct a separating hyperplane in quantum state space, and (2) a **quantum kernel estimator**, which uses the quantum device only to estimate the fidelity kernel K(x,z)=|<Phi(x)|Phi(z)>|^2 (via the all-zeros transition amplitude of U_Phi(z)^dag U_Phi(x)) and then solves the standard SVM dual on a classical computer.

**Experimental setup and results.** Both methods are run on two qubits of a 5-qubit superconducting transmon processor, with first-order zero-noise (Richardson) extrapolation for error mitigation. The data is *artificially engineered* on (0,2pi]^2 to be perfectly separable by the chosen feature map (labels from a random V in SU(4) and the parity Z1Z2, with a separation gap Delta=0.3), with 20 points per label. The variational classifier's accuracy rises with circuit depth, reaching ~100% for depths l>1 and surviving to l=4 (8 CNOTs) despite decoherence. The kernel estimator achieves 100% success on Sets I and II and 94.75% on Set III (the latter degraded by lower readout fidelity ~94% and a larger negative-eigenvalue kernel weight of 2.41). Training used 2000 shots; classification 10000; kernel entries 50000. Device metrics: CNOT error 0.0373, single-qubit errors ~1e-3, readout fidelity ~95%.

**Relevance to the hypothesis (high, with strong caveats).** The paper directly motivates the idea that quantum methods may excel where classical kernels struggle, and it carefully identifies the conditions: product-state and shallow (single-basis-change/IQP) feature maps are classically simulable and yield **no** advantage—indeed the authors give an explicit *dequantization* of the single-layer kernel (estimable to additive error eps via R~eps^-2 uniform samples). A potential advantage is claimed only for their two-basis-change map, whose kernel they **conjecture** (not prove) to be hard to estimate classically to additive polynomial error, by analogy to the hidden-shift/bent-function problem (an oracle query-complexity separation). Critically, the experiment demonstrates *hardware feasibility*, not a quantum advantage: no classical baseline is shown to fail on the (engineered, perfectly-separable) data, the advantage remains conjectural, and the demonstration uses only **2 qubits**—far below the >=20-qubit, classically-simulable regime of interest, and itself trivially simulable. The paper is thus best read as the conceptual blueprint (and the origin of the Qiskit ZZFeatureMap/QSVM) for the hypothesis, rather than as evidence that the hypothesis holds.

title: Machine learning of high dimensional data on a noisy quantum processor
authors: Evan Peters, Joao Caldeira, Alan Ho, Stefan Leichenauer, Masoud Mohseni, Hartmut Neven, Panagiotis Spentzouris, Doug Strain, Gabriel N. Perdue
one_line: Demonstrates a fidelity quantum-kernel SVM on Google's Sycamore superconducting processor that classifies real 67-dimensional supernova data with no dimensionality reduction, achieving test accuracy competitive with noiseless simulation and a classical RBF-SVM despite ~30% circuit fidelity.
methodology: Non-variational quantum kernel method: data are encoded via a hardware-efficient "Type 2" circuit U(x) (Hadamard + three single-qubit Z/Y/Z rotations per qubit, interleaved with native sqrt-iSWAP entanglers along a length-n path), and the kernel k(x_i,x_j)=|<phi(x_i)|phi(x_j)>|^2 is estimated by sampling the all-zeros bitstring probability of U_dagger(x_j)U(x_i). The resulting m x m kernel matrix is handed to a classical L2-penalized SVM with cross-validated hyperparameters (encoding scale c1, penalty C). The circuit is deliberately designed for large kernel magnitudes (median K >= 0.1) and decouples qubit count from the 67-dim input by encoding 3n features per layer in O(d/n) depth, plus readout-error mitigation via a Hamming-weight-truncated response matrix and an automated qubit-selection graph traversal.
datasets: PLAsTiCC (Photometric LSST Astronomical Time-series Classification Challenge) simulated supernova data from the Vera C. Rubin Observatory; binary classification of the two most-represented classes 42 (type II SN) vs 90 (type Ia SN). Time series engineered into 67-dimensional feature vectors with NO dimensionality reduction. Train/test split m=210, v=70 (balanced); dataset selected from a 1000x1000 simulated kernel matrix to match mean cross-validation performance. 5000 repetitions/circuit, ~1.83x10^8 experiments per qubit count.
baselines: Classical Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel SVM on identical 67-dim data subsets (main classical baseline, used in learning curves Fig. 3). Quantum baselines: noiseless Cirq wavefunction simulation of the same circuits; an alternative "Type 1" circuit (Havlicek/Farhi-Neven-style with PCA compression to n dims) shown to suffer vanishing kernels. Random guessing (0.5) as floor.
metrics: Classification accuracy (train, k-fold/LOOCV validation, reserved test); median kernel element magnitude |K_ij|; circuit fidelity (mean K_ii proxy); XEB/RB gate fidelities; statistical/relative error of sampled kernel.
key_results: Hardware QKM test accuracy ~0.63-0.70 across n=10,14,17 qubits, competitive with noiseless simulation (~0.71-0.73 test) despite circuit fidelity ~30%; noiseless train up to ~0.85-0.93. Classical RBF-SVM validation accuracy ~0.78 vs noiseless quantum ~0.78 on the learning curve (roughly comparable; classical fails to reach 100% train accuracy, as does quantum). Kernel magnitudes suppressed 50-70% by noise yet classifier robust due to SVM scale-invariance K->rK (hardware trends to r~0.29). Median |K_ij|>=0.1 for Type 2 (no vanishing) vs Type 1 vanishing as O(2^-n). ~50,000 shots/element needed to match noiseless cv accuracy; R=5000 incurs only ~1-2% accuracy loss. >90% of training points became support vectors.
qubits: Hardware: n in {10, 14, 17} qubits on Sycamore (23 active qubits, max 19 assignable with their path connectivity). Noiseless simulation used the same up to 17 qubits. Type 1 ablation simulated 2-17 qubits. Does NOT use >=20 qubits in this paper's own experiments (max 17); the authors cite a companion result (Huang et al., "Power of data", ref [4]) numerically validated on up to 30 qubits in TensorFlow Quantum, but that is not this paper's own work.
classical_struggle: No genuine quantum advantage is demonstrated or claimed. The paper explicitly states the circuits "are not candidates for demonstrating quantum advantage" because the Type 2 ansatz can be made arbitrarily shallow in qubit number (O(d/n) depth) and the c2->0 / small-angle limits are classically/trivially simulable. The reported quantum accuracy is at best comparable to (not exceeding) the classical RBF-SVM baseline; both fail to reach 100% accuracy, and the abstract only frames the supernova task as one "for which competitive classical classifiers fail to achieve 100% accuracy" - not one where classical methods fail and quantum wins. The main result is feasibility (handling real high-dim data without dimensionality reduction and robustness to noise), not separation. Any advantage is purely conjectured/aspirational: the conclusion names finding a "natural" dataset with classically-hard correlations (e.g., quantum many-body, quantum-sensor data) as the open problem, and defers the proposed advantage to the cited Huang et al. engineered-dataset/30-qubit TFQ work. Caveats: the relevant <=30-qubit simulable regime here is, by the authors' own admission, classically tractable; no dequantization is discussed but the easy-simulability of the shallow ansatz is the key limiting caveat. NOTE: despite the filename "projected_kernel_hardware," this paper uses a standard fidelity kernel |<phi|phi>|^2, NOT a projected quantum kernel.
code: No dedicated repository released. Built on Cirq (quantum simulation/execution) and scikit-learn (SVM); references TensorFlow Quantum (TFQ). Hardware accessed via Google Quantum Computing Service (Sycamore). Graph routines via NetworkX.
relevance: medium - It is a landmark NISQ-hardware quantum-kernel demonstration on real, high-dimensional (67-dim) data and is methodologically central to the quantum-kernel-SVM literature the hypothesis targets. However, relevance to the specific hypothesis (classical struggles, quantum via 20+ qubit simulation excels) is only partial: it uses at most 17 qubits (not >=20), runs primarily on hardware rather than simulation, deliberately uses a classically-simulable ansatz, and shows quantum merely comparable to (not beating) classical - explicitly disclaiming quantum advantage. Useful as a baseline/feasibility anchor and for circuit-design and noise-robustness lessons, and it points (via ref [4]) to the 30-qubit engineered-advantage work that more directly fits the hypothesis.

#### peters2021_projected_kernel_hardware

**Peters et al. (2021), "Machine learning of high dimensional data on a noisy quantum processor"** (FERMILAB-PUB-20-624-QIS; arXiv:2101.09581).

*Note on naming:* despite the local slug, this paper implements a **standard fidelity quantum kernel** k(x_i,x_j)=|<phi(x_i)|phi(x_j)>|^2, not a *projected* quantum kernel.

**Contribution.** The authors run a non-variational quantum-kernel SVM on Google's Sycamore superconducting processor to classify **real 67-dimensional supernova data** (PLAsTiCC type II vs type Ia, classes 42 vs 90) with **no dimensionality reduction** and **without vanishing kernel elements** - significantly higher input dimensionality than prior quantum-kernel hardware experiments.

**Method.** A hardware-efficient "Type 2" encoding circuit (Hadamard plus three single-qubit Z/Y/Z rotations per qubit, interleaved with native sqrt-iSWAP entanglers along a length-n path) maps 3n features per layer, encoding d-dimensional data in O(d/n) hardware depth and crucially **decoupling qubit count from data dimension**. Kernel elements are estimated from the all-zeros bitstring probability of U_dagger(x_j)U(x_i); the m x m matrix is passed to a classical L2-penalized SVM with cross-validated encoding scale c1 and penalty C. The ansatz is engineered for **large kernel magnitudes (median |K_ij| >= 0.1)**, avoiding the exponential O(2^-n) concentration that plagues the alternative "Type 1" (Havlicek/Farhi-Neven-style) circuit with PCA compression. Readout error is mitigated with a Hamming-weight-truncated response-matrix pseudo-inverse, and qubits are chosen by an automated calibration-aware graph-traversal selector.

**Setup & results.** Hardware runs used n in {10, 14, 17} qubits (Sycamore had 23 active, max 19 assignable), m=210 / v=70 train/test split, 5000 shots/circuit (~1.83x10^8 experiments per qubit count). Hardware **test accuracy ~0.63-0.70** is competitive with **noiseless simulation (~0.71-0.73 test)** and with a **classical RBF-SVM baseline (~0.78 validation on the learning curve)**, even at **circuit fidelity ~30%**. Robustness is attributed to the **scale-invariance of the SVM decision function** (K -> rK; hardware trends to r~0.29) - kernels are suppressed 50-70% by noise yet classification survives. Ablations show R=5000 shots cost only ~1-2% accuracy vs the infinite-shot limit (though ~50,000 shots are needed to fully match noiseless cv), and >90% of training points become support vectors under noise.

**Critical assessment for the hypothesis.** This paper does **not** demonstrate a task where classical methods fail and quantum wins, and it does not claim to. Quantum accuracy is at best **comparable to**, never exceeding, the classical RBF baseline; both classifiers fail to reach 100% accuracy on the task (the only sense in which classical "struggles"). The authors **explicitly disclaim quantum advantage**: the Type 2 ansatz is "arbitrarily shallow in number of qubits," and the small-angle / c2->0 limits make it classically (or trivially) simulable. Experiments use at most **17 qubits**, below the hypothesis's 20+ threshold, and run on hardware rather than large-scale simulation. Any advantage is **conjectured**: the open problem they pose is to find a "natural" dataset with classically hard-to-represent correlations (e.g., quantum many-body or quantum-sensor data), and they defer the actual proposed/numerically-validated advantage to the cited companion work (Huang et al., "Power of data," ref [4], validated on up to 30 qubits in TensorFlow Quantum). 

**Relevance: medium.** A landmark feasibility/baseline result for quantum-kernel SVMs on real high-dimensional data, valuable for circuit-design and noise-robustness lessons and as a pointer to the 30-qubit engineered-advantage line of work, but it neither uses >=20 qubits nor shows a classical-vs-quantum separation itself.

title: The power of quantum neural networks
authors: Amira Abbas, David Sutter, Christa Zoufal, Aurelien Lucchi, Alessio Figalli, Stefan Woerner
one_line: Introduces the Fisher-information-based "effective dimension" as a data-aware capacity measure, proves a generalisation bound for it, and uses it (plus the Fisher spectrum) to argue that a QNN with a hard-to-simulate feature map achieves higher effective dimension and faster/more resilient training than comparable classical neural networks.
methodology: Defines a QNN = data feature map U_x (Hadamard + RZ + higher-order RZZ encoding, depth 2, conjectured hard to simulate for depth>=2) followed by a parameterised RY/CNOT variational circuit G_theta with parity post-processing for binary classification. Uses information geometry: the (empirical) Fisher information matrix induces the "effective dimension" d_{gamma,n} (Def 3.1), for which the authors prove a novel generalisation bound (Thm 3.2, extended to non-full-rank Fisher via discretisation in Thm B.5). The Fisher eigenvalue spectrum is linked to barren plateaus (vanishing-gradient => eigenvalues concentrate at zero). Compares a "quantum neural network" (hard feature map), an "easy quantum model" (classically simulable angle encoding), and fully-connected classical feedforward nets matched on (#params d, input s_in, output s_out).
datasets: Iris (first two classes, 100 points) for the training experiments; scikit-learn make_blobs confusion sets (1000 points, increasing label randomisation 0-50%) to validate the effective dimension as a generalisation proxy. Capacity/Fisher-spectrum experiments are synthetic: parameters theta sampled uniformly on [-1,1]^d, Fisher computed 100 times per model with a standard Gaussian data prior. Configs: d<=100, s_in in {4,6,8,10}, s_out=2.
baselines: Classical: fully-connected feedforward neural networks over all topologies (with/without bias; ReLU, leaky-ReLU, tanh, sigmoid) for fixed d. Quantum: "easy quantum model" (classically simulable feature map) as an ablation. Capacity also cross-checked with the Fisher-Rao norm. No comparison to kernel methods or dequantized algorithms.
metrics: Normalised effective dimension (d_{gamma,n}/d), Fisher information eigenvalue spectrum/condition number, average training (cross-entropy) loss over 100 trials, and Fisher-Rao norm.
key_results: For d=40, s_in=4: the QNN attains the highest normalised effective dimension over a wide range of n and is the only model whose effective dimension never falls below the quantum models; classical Fisher spectrum has eigenvalues ~1e-14 (near machine zero) with a few large outliers, while the QNN spectrum is near-uniform and roughly constant as qubits grow. Iris training (d=8, ADAM, lr=0.1, 100 iters, avg of 100 trials): training loss QNN 23.14% vs classical 37.90% vs easy quantum 43.05%; Fisher-Rao norm QNN 117.84 vs easy 104.89 vs classical 46.45 (~250% higher than classical). On ibmq_montreal hardware (4 qubits, linear entanglement) the QNN trains even better than simulation, stabilising after ~33 iterations.
qubits: Simulation: S = s_in qubits with s_in in {4,6,8,10}, i.e. 4-10 qubits (all <20). Hardware: 4 qubits used on the 27-qubit ibmq_montreal device. No experiment reaches 20+ qubits.
classical_struggle: Only weakly and indirectly. The paper does NOT exhibit a concrete classification task where classical ML fails and quantum wins on accuracy; on Iris all models train, the QNN just reaches lower loss faster. The "advantage" is a capacity/trainability claim: the QNN has higher effective dimension and a non-degenerate, more uniform Fisher spectrum than matched classical nets, which empirically correlates with faster training and barren-plateau resilience. Key caveats: (1) the claimed power hinges entirely on the feature map being hard to classically simulate, which is only CONJECTURED (for depth>=2), not proven, and the easy (simulable) quantum model loses the advantage; (2) higher effective dimension is a double-edged proxy (more capacity can hurt generalisation), and the bound is a uniform-convergence upper bound, not a separation; (3) experiments use only 4-10 qubits, so nothing demonstrates the 20+ qubit regime; (4) no comparison to kernels or dequantization, no proof of any quantum speedup/learning separation. So: empirical capacity/trainability advantage at small scale, advantage conjectural, no proven task where classical provably fails.
code: No dedicated repository given; implemented in Qiskit (IBM Quantum Experience), classical nets standard. scikit-learn used for make_blobs/Iris.
relevance: High. It is a foundational, frequently-cited theoretical/empirical argument that a well-designed QNN can be "more powerful" (higher effective dimension, better-conditioned Fisher landscape, barren-plateau resilience) than comparable classical nets, directly addressing the hypothesis. Relevance is tempered for the specific "20+ qubit simulation where classical struggles" angle because experiments top out at 10 simulated / 4 hardware qubits and the advantage is capacity-based and conjectural rather than a demonstrated task-level classical failure.

#### abbas2021_power_qnn

**Abbas, Sutter, Zoufal, Lucchi, Figalli & Woerner (2021) — "The power of quantum neural networks."**

This influential paper asks whether quantum neural networks (QNNs) are genuinely *more powerful* than comparable classical neural networks, and answers via information geometry rather than VC-style arguments. The authors adopt the **effective dimension** d_{γ,n} — a data-dependent, Fisher-information-based capacity measure — and contribute a **novel generalisation bound** (Thm 3.2, extended to degenerate/non-full-rank Fisher matrices via a discretisation argument in Thm B.5). They additionally connect the **Fisher eigenvalue spectrum to the barren-plateau phenomenon**: a model in a barren plateau has Fisher eigenvalues concentrating at zero, so a spectrum that stays spread out signals trainability.

**Models.** The QNN uses the second-order feature map of Havlíček et al. (Hadamard + RZ + RZZ, depth 2), *conjectured hard to simulate classically for depth ≥ 2*, followed by an RY/CNOT variational form and parity post-processing. It is benchmarked against (i) matched fully-connected classical feedforward nets (all topologies, several activations) and (ii) an "easy quantum model" ablation that swaps in a classically simulable angle-encoding feature map. Models are matched on parameter count d (≤100), input size s_in ∈ {4,6,8,10}, and output size s_out = 2.

**Key findings.** For d=40, s_in=4, the QNN attains the **highest normalised effective dimension across all finite data sizes**, with a near-uniform Fisher spectrum that stays roughly constant as qubits grow; the classical model's spectrum is highly degenerate (eigenvalues ~10⁻¹⁴ with a few large outliers), and the easy quantum model is intermediate and degrades with qubit count. On Iris (d=8, ADAM, 100 iters, avg of 100 trials) the QNN reaches the lowest training loss **23.14%** vs **37.90%** (classical) and **43.05%** (easy quantum), with the highest Fisher-Rao norm (**117.84** vs 46.45 classical, ~250% higher). The advantage persists — indeed improves — when trained on real hardware using **4 qubits on the 27-qubit ibmq_montreal device**.

**Critical assessment for the hypothesis (classical-hard / quantum-wins).** The demonstrated advantage is one of **capacity and trainability**, not a task where classical ML provably fails: on Iris all three models train and the QNN merely converges faster/lower. Crucially, the entire advantage rests on the feature map being **hard to simulate classically — a conjecture, not a proof** — and the simulable "easy quantum model" forfeits the advantage, underscoring that encoding choice, not quantumness per se, drives the result. Higher effective dimension is also a double-edged proxy (more capacity can harm generalisation), and the central theorem is a uniform-convergence upper bound rather than a learning separation. Finally, **all experiments use only 4–10 simulated qubits (and 4 on hardware)**, so the paper offers no evidence in the 20+ qubit regime central to this review and includes no comparison to quantum/classical kernels or dequantization. **Relevance: high** as a foundational motivation that well-designed QNNs *can* out-express and out-train matched classical nets, but the claimed advantage is empirical, small-scale, and conjectural rather than a proven classically-intractable task.

title: The Inductive Bias of Quantum Kernels
authors: Jonas M. Kübler, Simon Buchholz, Bernhard Schölkopf
one_line: A spectral analysis of quantum kernels showing that an expressive (exponentially large) quantum feature space destroys generalization, so a quantum advantage in kernel learning requires encoding a problem-specific inductive bias that is classically hard to encode — which the authors argue is plausible only for quantum-generated data, not generic classical datasets.
methodology: The paper studies kernel ridge regression with quantum kernels k(x,x')=Tr[ρ(x)ρ(x')] through the spectrum (Mercer eigenvalues γ_i and eigenfunctions φ_i) of the integral operator, relating learnability to kernel-target alignment and task-model alignment. They prove (Theorem 1) that if the per-coordinate mean-embedding purity Tr[ρ_μi^2] ≤ δ < 1 as qubit count d grows and sample size is polynomial in d, the largest eigenvalue γ_max ≤ sqrt(Tr[ρ_μ^2]) shrinks exponentially and no function can be learned. They then analyze 'projected/biased' kernels q^V_m on m qubits (partial trace over the rest); for Haar-random V (Theorem 2) the reduced kernel concentrates to 2^-m + O(2^-d/2), giving one large constant eigenvalue (1/2) and three exponentially small eigenvalues O(2^-d) whose eigenfunctions are conjectured classically hard. Experiments simulate the full quantum state for d=1..7 qubits to validate the spectrum and learning behavior.
datasets: Synthetic/engineered only. Data drawn from the d-dimensional uniform distribution on [0,2π]^d, N=200 samples (2/3 train, 1/3 test). Labels y = c·f*(x) + ε with f*(x)=Tr[ρ̃^V_1(x)σ_z] generated by measuring observable M*=σ_z on the first qubit after a fixed random unitary V, with Gaussian noise Var[ε]=10^-4 and c set so Var[f(X)]=1. No real/classical datasets used.
baselines: Full quantum kernel k (unbiased, x→single-qubit RX embedding + random unitary V); biased/projected quantum kernel q = q^V_1 (correct bias, first qubit); wrongly-biased quantum kernel q_w (second qubit, wrong bias); classical RBF kernel k_rbf(x,x')=exp(-||x-x'||^2/2). All evaluated via kernel ridge regression.
metrics: Mean squared error (train/test), Mercer eigenvalue magnitudes γ_i, kernel-target alignment A(k,f), task-model alignment C(i).
key_results: Biased kernel q (with the correct single-qubit bias) achieves near-optimal MSE (~10^-4, the noise floor) for all d=1..7 and its first ~4 spectral components fully capture f* (task-model alignment C(i)→1 within 4 components; kernel alignment A concentrated around 0.6 for d=7). The full quantum kernel k and classical RBF k_rbf overfit: low train error but test MSE climbing toward ~1 (≈Var[f]) as d grows. The wrongly-biased q_w underfits (train≈test error, ~1). Spectrum of q matches theory: γ_0≈1/2 (constant eigenfunction), γ_1,2,3 = O(2^-d). The advantage is the gap between q's flat low test error and the other models' test error rising to O(1) with increasing qubits.
qubits: Simulation only (full state vector), d = 1 to 7 qubits. No quantum hardware. No experiments at >=20 qubits; theoretical claims are asymptotic in d but empirical demonstration is limited to <=7 qubits.
classical_struggle: Partial and heavily caveated. The paper does construct a task where the classical RBF kernel (and the unbiased quantum kernel) fail to generalize while the correctly biased quantum kernel succeeds — but this is by construction, not a demonstration of generic classical hardness. The 'advantage' rests on an inductive-bias argument: the eigenfunctions φ_1,2,3 of the projected kernel are *conjectured* (citing Bremner et al.-type IQP hardness, ref [48]) to be exponentially hard to compute classically, so a classical model cannot bias toward them; this is conjectured, not proven. Two strong caveats undercut a usable advantage: (1) the favorable eigenvalues are O(2^-d), so Theorem 2a implies estimating the kernel from measurements requires exponentially many shots — a kernel-analogue of barren plateaus — so the simulated advantage is unlikely to survive measurement-based evaluation on hardware; (2) the bias must be known a priori (one must know f* = Tr[ρ̃^V_1(x)M*]), which is unrealistic for real classical datasets. The empirical win is shown only at <=7 qubits (simulable). The genuinely proven classical-hardness example they cite is the discrete-log task of Liu et al. (separate paper), which needs a fault-tolerant computer. Net message of the paper itself is 'sobering': they conclude there is no indication QML will substantially improve supervised learning on classical datasets; advantage is plausible mainly for quantum-generated data. No dequantization is performed, but the centering trick they note means the only classically-encodable bias (the constant function) is the one a classical model can already exploit.
code: Yes — https://github.com/jmkuebler/quantumbias (builds on standard open-source packages, refs [50,51]; full-state simulation).
relevance: High. The paper directly addresses the hypothesis (tasks where classical struggles and quantum wins) and provides a rigorous spectral framework for *when* such an advantage can exist. It is a crucial cautionary/conditions paper: it shows the advantage requires a classically-inexpressible inductive bias, demonstrates it only in a constructed synthetic task at <=7 simulated qubits, and identifies the exponential-measurement obstruction. Essential for critically scoping any '20+ qubit simulation' claim of advantage on classical data.

#### kubler2021_inductive_bias_quantum_kernels

**Kübler, Buchholz & Schölkopf (2021), "The Inductive Bias of Quantum Kernels" (NeurIPS 2021).**

This paper provides a spectral-theoretic account of *when* quantum kernel methods can beat classical ones, framed through the classical lens of inductive bias rather than raw expressivity. Working with kernel ridge regression over the quantum kernel k(x,x') = Tr[ρ(x)ρ(x')], the authors decompose the integral operator into Mercer eigenvalues γ_i and eigenfunctions φ_i and tie learnability to kernel-target alignment and task-model alignment.

Their central negative result (**Theorem 1**) is that a quantum kernel built from a sufficiently expressive embedding cannot generalize: if the per-coordinate mean-embedding purity Tr[ρ_μi²] ≤ δ < 1 as the number of qubits d grows and the sample size is only polynomial in d, then the largest eigenvalue is bounded by γ_max ≤ √(Tr[ρ_μ²]) and shrinks exponentially, so *no* target function can be learned (risk ≥ (1-ε)‖f‖²). An exponentially large feature space therefore implies a vanishing inductive bias — the same generalization failure classical kernels would suffer without bandwidth control.

To recover an advantage they study *projected/biased* kernels q^V_m on m qubits (partial trace over the rest). For a Haar-random unitary V, **Theorem 2** shows the reduced kernel concentrates to 2^-m + O(2^-d/2), with one large eigenvalue (≈1/2 for m=1, a constant eigenfunction) and three exponentially small eigenvalues O(2^-d) whose eigenfunctions are *conjectured* to be classically hard to compute. A classical model cannot bias toward those functions, whereas the quantum model can — the proposed route to advantage.

**Experiments (simulation only, d = 1–7 qubits, full state vector).** On an engineered synthetic task where labels come from measuring σ_z on the first qubit (f*(x)=Tr[ρ̃^V_1(x)σ_z], N=200, noise Var=10⁻⁴), the correctly-biased kernel q attains near-optimal test MSE (~10⁻⁴) flat across all d, with its first ~4 spectral components fully capturing f* (task-model alignment → 1; kernel-target alignment concentrated ~0.6 at d=7). In contrast, the full quantum kernel k and the classical RBF kernel k_rbf overfit (test MSE rising toward Var[f]≈1 as d increases), and a wrongly-biased kernel q_w underfits. This is the paper's concrete "classical struggles, quantum wins" demonstration.

**Critical assessment for the hypothesis.** The advantage is real but constructed and conditional, not a generic classical-hardness result. (i) The classical hardness of the favorable eigenfunctions is *conjectured* (IQP-type), not proven. (ii) The required bias must be known a priori — one must already know the data came from a single-qubit observable — which is unrealistic for real classical datasets. (iii) The favorable eigenvalues are O(2⁻ᵈ), so Theorem 2a implies kernel estimation needs exponentially many measurements (a barren-plateau analogue); the advantage seen in exact simulation likely does not survive shot-based evaluation on hardware. (iv) The only classically-encodable bias here (the constant function via data centering) is already available to classical models. The empirical demonstration is confined to ≤7 simulable qubits — there is no ≥20-qubit study. The authors' own conclusion is deliberately sobering: a quantum advantage on classical datasets appears hard to come by, and the more plausible setting is data generated by a quantum process. Code: https://github.com/jmkuebler/quantumbias.

**Relevance: High** — a foundational "conditions and caveats" paper that sharpens what a 20+-qubit simulated advantage would actually require, and warns that expressivity alone (and even exact-simulation success) does not establish a usable quantum advantage.

title: Supervised quantum machine learning models are kernel methods
one_line: A technical manuscript proving that essentially all supervised quantum models (data-encoding circuit + measurement) are mathematically equivalent to classical kernel methods whose kernel is computed on a quantum computer, so the data-encoding strategy (which fixes the kernel) is the only ingredient that can possibly separate quantum from classical ML.
methodology: Defines the data-encoding feature map x -> rho(x) = |phi(x)><phi(x)| into the space of density matrices with the Hilbert-Schmidt inner product, yielding the quantum kernel kappa(x,x') = tr[rho(x')rho(x)] = |<phi(x')|phi(x)>|^2. Proves quantum models f(x)=tr[rho(x)M] are linear models in this feature space and that the space of quantum models equals the RKHS of the quantum kernel. Via the representer theorem, optimal measurements for regularised empirical risk minimisation are kernel expansions in the M training points (M = sum_m alpha_m rho(x^m)), reducing training to an M-dimensional convex program (a support vector machine with hinge loss). Also gives a Fourier-series characterisation of quantum kernels for Pauli/time-evolution encodings.
datasets: None used for empirical evaluation. The paper is theoretical; illustrative figures (Figs. 6-8) plot kernel functions for a single-qubit Pauli-X / rotation embedding over toy 1D-2D synthetic inputs (e.g., equidistant points on an interval, x in R^2 with one point fixed), generated via PennyLane simulations. A referenced (not included) PennyLane demo uses ~100 data samples with 10-20 parameters for timing.
baselines: Conceptual comparison between kernel-based training (SVM / convex program over M alpha-coefficients) and variational circuit training (parametrised ansatz over K theta-parameters via parameter-shift gradients). Also contrasts with classical neural networks (O(M) training scaling) and classical kernel methods / random-feature approximations. No accuracy benchmark tables.
metrics: Theoretical/asymptotic metrics only: degrees of freedom of optimal measurement (M vs O(2^(2n))), training complexity (kernel: O(M^2) classically, O(M) with fault-tolerant quantum linear algebra; variational: O(|theta|M) circuit evaluations), convexity / global-optimality guarantees, qubit counts per encoding (O(n)), and kernel smoothness via Fourier-frequency content. No precision/recall/accuracy.
key_results: (1) Optimal quantum-model measurements have only M degrees of freedom rather than O(2^(2n)). (2) Kernel-based training is guaranteed to find better-or-equal minima than variational training (M_opt_alpha = M_opt), and is convex (single global minimum) for convex losses, avoiding barren plateaus. (3) Training reduces to an M-dimensional convex problem solvable in O(M^2); fault-tolerant quantum linear algebra could in principle bring this to O(M). (4) Encoding-to-kernel table: basis -> Kronecker delta; amplitude -> |x^dagger x'|^2; repeated amplitude -> (...)^r; rotation -> prod cos(x_k - x'_k)^2; coherent-state -> Gaussian kernel e^{-|x-x'|^2}; general near-term -> Fourier series. (5) Empirical note (from referenced demo): with 10-20 params and ~100 samples, kernel-based training is in fact much faster than variational training on hardware.
qubits: Theoretical: encodings use O(n) qubits (basis, amplitude, rotation) or O(rn) (repeated amplitude); general feature space is 2^n x 2^n. No specific hardware runs and no simulations at fixed large qubit counts; toy illustrations are single-qubit (n=1) or few-qubit. No experiments at >=20 qubits.
classical_struggle: Does NOT empirically demonstrate any task where classical methods fail and a simulable quantum method wins. The paper's thesis actually constrains quantum advantage: because every quantum model is a classical kernel method, the ONLY possible separation is a quantum kernel that is classically hard to compute. Schuld explicitly states the existence of such a kernel relies on a conjectured classically-hard computation (ref. [11], Liu-Arunachalam-Temme) and stresses "it is still unclear whether any useful applications turn out to be enabled solely by quantum computers" and "the question of quantum kernels that are actually useful for every-day problems is still wide open." Any advantage is therefore conjectured/conditional, not proven or empirically shown here. Critically, many highlighted encodings dequantize trivially: amplitude encoding gives |x^dagger x'|^2 (essentially a linear kernel), rotation gives a classical cosine kernel, and coherent-state encoding gives the classical Gaussian kernel - all efficiently classically computable, hence NO advantage. The quantum-training speedup (O(M)) requires fault-tolerant hardware and strong data-loading-oracle (QRAM) assumptions, and is a runtime claim, not a learning-capability claim. Caveat for the hypothesis: separation would require a kernel that is hard to simulate classically, which generally precludes the <=30-qubit exact-simulation regime; so this paper offers no support for a simulable (<=30 qubit) quantum-wins task and arguably argues against expecting one.
code: No paper-specific repository. Uses PennyLane (Bergholm et al. 2018, ref. [23]) for kernel-plot simulations; references a public PennyLane demo on kernel-based training (pennylane.ai/qml/demos/tutorial_kernel_based_training.html).
relevance: High - this is a foundational/conceptual anchor for the hypothesis, but as a cautionary one. It rigorously frames WHERE any quantum-vs-classical ML separation can come from (only a classically-hard-to-compute data-encoding kernel) and makes clear that such separations are conjectural and that common encodings are dequantizable. It is essential context for critically scoping claims of "quantum excels where classical struggles," though it provides no positive evidence of a simulable quantum advantage.

#### schuld2021_models_are_kernels

**Schuld, M. (2021). "Supervised quantum machine learning models are kernel methods." arXiv:2101.11020.**

This technical manuscript provides the theoretical backbone for reasoning about quantum-versus-classical separations in supervised QML. Schuld shows that essentially every supervised quantum model - a data-encoding circuit producing the state rho(x) = |phi(x)><phi(x)| followed by a measurement M, with prediction f(x) = tr[rho(x)M] - is exactly a *linear model* in the feature space of density matrices equipped with the Hilbert-Schmidt inner product. The induced "quantum kernel" is kappa(x,x') = tr[rho(x')rho(x)] = |<phi(x')|phi(x)>|^2. A central structural result is that the space of quantum models coincides with the reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) of this kernel. Consequently, via the representer theorem, the optimal measurement under regularised empirical risk minimisation is a kernel expansion in the training data, M_opt = sum_m alpha_m rho(x^m), with only M (number of training points) degrees of freedom rather than the O(2^(2n)) of a general n-qubit observable. Training reduces to an M-dimensional **convex** program - a support vector machine with a quantum kernel - which is guaranteed to reach better-or-equal minima than variational circuit training and sidesteps barren plateaus, at the cost of O(M^2) pairwise-distance computations.

**Methodology and evidence.** The contribution is purely theoretical, supported by a sequence of theorems (linear-model equivalence, RKHS equivalence, representer theorem, convexity) and a Fourier-series characterisation of quantum kernels for Pauli / time-evolution encodings. Illustrations are toy single- or few-qubit embeddings (e.g., Pauli-X rotation giving a cos^2((x-x')/2) kernel) plotted with PennyLane. There are no accuracy benchmarks, no datasets beyond synthetic 1D-2D toy inputs, and no hardware or large-scale simulation experiments; no runs at >=20 qubits.

**Relevance to the hypothesis (high, but cautionary).** The paper directly addresses "tasks where classical struggles but quantum excels" by *constraining* where such an advantage could come from: since any quantum model equals a classical kernel method plus a quantum kernel, "the separation between classical and quantum machine learning lies only in the ability of quantum computers to implement classically hard kernels." Crucially, this paper does **not** demonstrate such a task. Schuld is explicit that the only known classically-hard kernel (citing Liu-Arunachalam-Temme and Huang et al.) rests on a *conjectured* classically-hard computation, and that "it is still unclear whether any useful applications turn out to be enabled solely by quantum computers" and "the question of quantum kernels that are actually useful for every-day problems is still wide open." Many of the encodings surveyed are explicitly dequantizable: amplitude encoding yields |x^dagger x'|^2 (a linear kernel), rotation encoding yields a classical cosine kernel, and coherent-state encoding yields the classical Gaussian kernel - none of which offer any advantage. The separate O(M)-time quantum *training* speedup requires fault-tolerant hardware and strong data-loading (QRAM) oracle assumptions and is a runtime, not a learning-capability, claim. For the present hypothesis this implies a sobering caveat: a genuine quantum-wins separation requires a kernel that is hard to simulate classically, which generally lies outside the exact <=30-qubit simulation regime. Thus this paper supplies essential framing and rigor for scoping quantum-advantage claims, but offers no positive evidence of a simulable (<=30 qubit) task where classical methods fail and quantum methods win.

title: Covariant quantum kernels for data with group structure
authors: Jennifer R. Glick, Tanvi P. Gujarati, Antonio D. Córcoles, Youngseok Kim, Abhinav Kandala, Jay M. Gambetta, Kristan Temme (IBM Quantum)
one_line: Introduces covariant quantum kernels for data with group structure—built from a unitary group representation and an optimizable fiducial state—and demonstrates them on a 27-qubit superconducting processor for a coset-labeling classification task.
methodology: The covariant quantum kernel maps a datum x in a group G to a feature state Dx|ψ⟩⟨ψ|Dx† via a unitary representation Dx and a fiducial state |ψ⟩=V|0^n⟩, giving the fidelity kernel K(x,x̃)=|⟨ψ|Dx†Dx̃|ψ⟩|^2 estimated by quantum kernel estimation (QKE, transition-amplitude to |0^n⟩). The fiducial state is parameterized by a variational circuit Vλ and optimized via weighted kernel alignment (SPSA min-max of the SVM dual cost) so the kernel best separates the data. The kernel feeds a standard SVM for binary classification. They specialize this to the "labeling cosets with error" (LCE) benchmark with G=SU(2)^⊗27 and a graph-stabilizer subgroup matching the device coupling graph.
datasets: Synthetic/engineered only. (1) LCE benchmark: two left-cosets C± = c±·S_graph of G=SU(2)^⊗27 with c± randomly drawn in [-π/2,π/2]^{2n}; each datum perturbed with Gaussian Euler-angle noise of variance ε=0.01; training set 10 points per label (20 total) for kernel alignment, test set 50 points per label (100 total). (2) Theoretical DLOG dataset of Liu et al. [16] over Z_p* (reviewed, not run): bit-strings labeled by discrete-log intervals. No real-world data.
baselines: Within-quantum comparisons: error-mitigated vs. unmitigated 27-qubit kernel estimation; trivial fiducial state |0^n⟩ (identity kernel K=δ_{x,x̃}) vs. aligned/subgroup-invariant graph-state fiducial state, illustrating the alignment gain. Conceptual classical baseline: classical group-data kernels (e.g., Kondor/ranking kernels) noted as expensive and usually truncated to low Fourier weights. No head-to-head numeric classical ML baseline (e.g., RBF-SVM) is reported on LCE.
metrics: Binary classification test accuracy (% success), kernel-alignment cost function F(α,λ), and convergence of fiducial parameter λ toward ideal π/2 across SPSA steps.
key_results: Both mitigated and unmitigated 27-qubit experiments reach 100% test-set classification success after SPSA step 13; the mitigated version reaches high accuracy faster and converges λ→π/2 (ideal) more quickly with lower alignment cost. Cost flattens after ~11 SPSA trials. 21 SPSA trial steps total; each circuit sampled 8,192 times. No readout error mitigation was applied, yet thresholded kernels remained robust. Authors note this is, to date, the largest circuit width (27 qubits) used for a quantum kernel.
qubits: 27 qubits on hardware (ibmq_kolkata, heavy-hexagon), G=SU(2)^⊗27. This is >=20 qubits. The authors explicitly state the circuit is shallow enough to be classically simulable via 2D tensor-network methods [37], so 27 qubits here does NOT imply classical intractability.
classical_struggle: Partial / qualified. The paper does NOT empirically demonstrate a task where classical ML fails and simulable quantum wins. The only PROVEN classical-hard/quantum-easy separation is the reviewed DLOG/Z_p* construction of Liu et al. [16] (Appendix B.1): conditional on the classical hardness of discrete log, no classical learner beats 1/2+1/poly(n) while the covariant quantum kernel achieves near-perfect accuracy. That separation is super-polynomial-scaling and inherently NOT simulable with few qubits—it is a hardness-based asymptotic claim, not the 27-qubit experiment. The actual 27-qubit LCE experiment is an engineered benchmark explicitly chosen so it can be solved to zero error AND is acknowledged by the authors to be classically simulable (shallow, 2D topology), so it provides NO quantum advantage—it is a hardware/method validation, not an advantage demonstration. Caveats: advantage rests on the unproven DLOG hardness assumption; classical group-data kernels could plausibly also solve LCE; advantage disappears once circuits are deep/non-2D where simulation becomes hard but that regime is not tested. So under the hypothesis's "<=30-qubit simulable" lens, this paper does not show a win; it argues group-structured data is a promising candidate family.
code: none (no repository or framework linked in the read chunks; experiments on IBM Quantum hardware with SPSA-based kernel alignment).
relevance: High relevance to the hypothesis as a methodological and conceptual anchor: it defines a structured class (group/coset data) argued to be a natural fit for quantum advantage and connects to a proven hardness-based separation. But for the specific simulable (<=30-qubit) "quantum wins where classical fails" claim, its evidence is LOW—the 27-qubit demo is explicitly simulable and on a fabricated zero-error task with no classical baseline beaten.
