#### glick2021_covariant_kernels

**Glick, Gujarati, Córcoles, Kim, Kandala, Gambetta & Temme (IBM Quantum), "Covariant quantum kernels for data with group structure" (arXiv:2105.03406, 2021; Nature Physics 2024).**

*Contribution.* This work introduces **covariant quantum kernels**, a family of quantum kernels tailored to data living in (a subset of) a group, X ⊆ G. A datum x is embedded via a unitary group representation Dx acting on a fiducial state |ψ⟩ = V|0^n⟩, yielding the feature state Φ(x) = Dx|ψ⟩⟨ψ|Dx† and the left-invariant fidelity kernel K(x,x̃) = |⟨ψ|Dx†Dx̃|ψ⟩|², estimated on hardware by quantum kernel estimation (the |0^n⟩→|0^n⟩ transition probability of V†Dx†Dx̃V). A central insight is that the *fiducial state* is the key degree of freedom: a poor choice (e.g. |0^n⟩) collapses the kernel to the useless identity matrix K = δ_{x,x̃}, while a subgroup-invariant state yields perfect separation. When no prior structure is known, |ψ⟩ is learned via a variational circuit Vλ optimized through **weighted kernel alignment** (an SPSA-driven min-max over the SVM dual objective).

*Method & experiment.* The authors define a benchmark, **"labeling cosets with error" (LCE)**: data are drawn from two left-cosets C± = c±·S of a group and perturbed by small noise (ε = 0.01), and the learner must classify coset membership. They realize this with G = SU(2)^⊗27 and a graph-stabilizer subgroup matched to the device coupling graph, running on the 27-qubit heavy-hexagon processor *ibmq_kolkata* — to their knowledge the largest circuit width used for a quantum kernel at the time. Kernel alignment uses 10 points/label for training and 50 points/label for testing, 21 SPSA steps, 8,192 shots/circuit, with and without error mitigation.

*Results.* Both mitigated and unmitigated runs reach **100% test accuracy after SPSA step 13**, with the variational parameter λ converging to its ideal value π/2; error mitigation accelerates convergence and lowers the alignment cost. The experiment is primarily a high-fidelity hardware/method validation rather than a benchmark of accuracy against classical learners.

*Critical assessment of classical-vs-quantum separation.* The only **proven** advantage cited is the discrete-logarithm construction of Liu et al. over Z_p* (reviewed in Appendix B.1), shown to be a special case of the covariant kernel: conditional on DLOG hardness, no classical learner exceeds ½ + 1/poly(n) while the quantum kernel achieves near-perfect accuracy. Crucially, this is an **asymptotic, hardness-assumption-based, super-polynomial-scaling** separation — it is *not* the 27-qubit experiment, and it is inherently *not* classically simulable. The actual hardware demonstration (LCE) is an **engineered, zero-error benchmark** that the authors themselves acknowledge is **classically simulable**: the circuit is "sufficiently shallow so that classical simulation methods can be applied," exploiting the explicit 2D coupling structure (an advantage that "disappears for deeper circuits or other coupling topologies"). No classical ML baseline (e.g. RBF-SVM or a classical group kernel) is run on LCE; the classical-difficulty argument is only that advanced classical group kernels are expensive and usually Fourier-truncated.

*Relevance to the hypothesis.* **High conceptual relevance, low empirical support for the simulable-advantage claim.** The paper makes a strong case that **group-/coset-structured data is a principled candidate domain** for quantum kernel advantage and ties it to a rigorous hardness-based separation. However, it does **not** exhibit a task where classical methods empirically fail and a *simulable* (≤30-qubit) quantum method wins: its 27-qubit instance is explicitly simulable and lacks a defeated classical baseline, while its provable separation lives in the non-simulable asymptotic regime under an unproven hardness assumption. It is best used as a methodological foundation (covariant kernels + kernel alignment + structured data), not as evidence of practical quantum advantage at small qubit counts.

title: Quantum Machine Learning Beyond Kernel Methods
authors: Sofiene Jerbi, Lukas J. Fiderer, Hendrik Poulsen Nautrup, Jonas M. Kübler, Hans J. Briegel, Vedran Dunjko
one_line: Unifies explicit, implicit (kernel), and data re-uploading quantum models as linear models in quantum feature spaces, and proves exponential resource separations (in qubits and data) showing that data re-uploading and explicit models can dramatically outperform quantum kernel methods, which overfit despite the representer theorem's training-loss guarantee.
methodology: The authors recast all parametrized-quantum-circuit models as linear models f(x)=Tr[rho(x)O] in a quantum feature space, and give an exact mapping (via gate teleportation / nested gadgets) from any D-encoding-gate data re-uploading model to an equivalent explicit linear model using O(D log(D/delta')) additional qubits, plus an Omega(D) lower bound proving optimality. They then use learning-theory dimension arguments (Hsu's theorem) on the concept class of k-sparse parity functions to derive qubit/data lower bounds for linear vs. implicit models, and an explicit single-qubit data re-uploading construction as the upper bound. Finally they run a numerical regression experiment (TensorFlow Quantum) on quantum-relabeled fashion-MNIST comparing explicit, implicit, and classical models.
datasets: (1) Theoretical task: k-sparse parity functions g_A(x)=prod_{i in A} x_i on x in {-1,1}^d, with uniform and Daniely-Malach mixture input distributions; parametrized by input dimension d. (2) Numerical task: fashion-MNIST (28x28 grayscale clothing images) reduced via PCA to n-dimensional vectors (2<=n<=12), re-labeled by a quantum explicit model (Havlicek encoding + hardware-efficient ansatz, Pauli-Z label). Training set M=1000, validation 100, test 100; 10 random labeling functions per system size.
baselines: Quantum: explicit (variational) models, implicit/quantum-kernel models (kernel ridge regression), and data re-uploading models. Classical (matched to Huang et al. 2021): two-layer feed-forward neural network (MLPRegressor), linear kernel SVR/KernelRidge, Gaussian/RBF kernel SVR/KernelRidge, random forest, gradient boosting, AdaBoost; all grid-searched/hyperparametrized via scikit-learn.
metrics: Mean-squared error (regression training loss and test/expected loss); resource metrics: number of qubits n and number of training samples M needed to reach expected error epsilon; spectral-norm overhead and additional-qubit count for model mappings.
key_results: Parity-task separation (Theorem 2 / G.6) for expected MSE epsilon<1/2: any linear quantum model needs n >= d/4 + (1/2)log2(1-2epsilon) qubits = Omega(d); any implicit (kernel) model additionally needs M >= 2^{d/2}(1-2epsilon) = Omega(2^d) samples; a single-qubit data re-uploading model (depth 2d+1) achieves perfect expected loss with prob 1-delta using only M = 32 log(2d/delta) = O(log d) samples. Exact data-reuploading-to-explicit mapping costs O(D log(D/delta')) extra qubits, with a matching Omega(D) lower bound (Corollary 1 / G.7). Numerical experiment: implicit models reach ~0 training loss at all sizes but generalize poorly; on the quantum-tailored fashion-MNIST task, a clear test-loss separation appears from n=7 qubits onward, where classical models become competitive with implicit/kernel models but explicit (variational) models clearly outperform both.
qubits: Numerical simulations span n=2 to 12 qubits (no hardware; TensorFlow Quantum statevector simulation; no run uses >=20 qubits). Theoretical separations are stated asymptotically in d (qubits scale as Omega(d), unbounded). Data re-uploading construction for parities uses a single qubit.
classical_struggle: Partial/qualified. The headline rigorous separations are between quantum model types (data re-uploading vs explicit/linear vs implicit kernel), not strictly quantum-vs-classical: the parity concept class used is explicitly noted to be classically easy (a depth-2 neural network learns it, and parities are solvable classically), so it does NOT demonstrate a quantum-over-classical advantage. The genuine quantum-vs-classical evidence is the empirical fashion-MNIST experiment (<=12 qubits, fully simulable), where explicit quantum models beat all tuned classical baselines from n=7 onward; however this is on a deliberately quantum-tailored/quantum-relabeled task (labels generated by a quantum explicit model with the Havlicek encoding conjectured-but-not-proven classically intractable), it is empirical not proven, advantage is in test MSE not a hardness result, and authors caution about trainability obstacles (barren plateaus for explicit, exponential kernel concentration for implicit) requiring exponentially many measurements. The paper's main rigorous classical-relevant message is a negative one about kernels: quantum (and classical) kernel methods provably need data linear in concept-class dimension (Omega(2^d)) and overfit despite regularization, dequantifying the apparent kernel advantage. No dequantization of the explicit-model advantage is shown, but no separation is proven to require quantum hardware either.
code: TensorFlow Quantum; code at https://github.com/sjerbi/QML-beyond-kernel (classical baselines via scikit-learn, ADAM optimizer).
relevance: High. Directly central to the hypothesis: it studies, both theoretically and via <=12-qubit simulation, tasks where quantum (especially data re-uploading and explicit) models outperform alternatives, and empirically shows a quantum-over-classical test-error gap on a simulable task. Importantly for the hypothesis, it is a sharp cautionary reference: it shows the parity separations are classically easy (so not a quantum advantage), shows quantum kernel methods overfit/require exponential data, and stresses that the favorable empirical task is quantum-tailored with only conjectured classical hardness and unresolved trainability caveats.

#### jerbi2023_beyond_kernel

**Quantum Machine Learning Beyond Kernel Methods** (Jerbi, Fiderer, Poulsen Nautrup, Kübler, Briegel, Dunjko, 2023).

**Contribution.** This paper provides a unifying framework that expresses all standard parametrized-quantum-circuit models — explicit (variational) models, implicit (quantum kernel) models, and data re-uploading models — as *linear models* f(x)=Tr[rho(x)O] in a quantum feature space. Its central technical result is an *exact* mapping from any data re-uploading model with D encoding gates to an equivalent explicit linear model, using O(D log(D/delta')) additional qubits via (nested) gate-teleportation gadgets, together with a matching Omega(D) lower bound (Corollary 1 / Theorem G.7) proving the mapping is essentially optimal.

**Resource separations.** Using dimension-counting arguments (Hsu's theorem) on the concept class of k-sparse parity functions g_A(x)=prod_{i in A} x_i over {-1,1}^d, the authors prove (Theorem 2 / G.6) that to reach expected MSE epsilon<1/2: (i) any linear quantum model needs n >= d/4 + (1/2)log2(1-2epsilon) = Omega(d) qubits; (ii) any *implicit/kernel* model additionally needs M >= 2^{d/2}(1-2epsilon) = Omega(2^d) data samples; whereas (iii) a *single-qubit* data re-uploading model (depth 2d+1) achieves perfect expected loss with probability 1-delta using only M = 32 log(2d/delta) = O(log d) samples. This yields exponential separations in both qubits (explicit/re-uploading over implicit) and data (re-uploading over kernel methods).

**Reinterpreting the representer theorem.** A key conceptual message is that the representer theorem only guarantees lower *regularized training* loss for kernel models; the authors show this expressivity causes severe *overfitting* and poor generalization. Their bit-string and gate-teleportation constructions yield kernels that collapse to (near-)Kronecker-delta functions, so the corresponding implicit model is classically simulable yet fails to generalize — directly dequantifying the apparent kernel advantage.

**Numerical evidence.** On a "quantum-tailored" regression task (fashion-MNIST PCA-reduced to n dims, 2<=n<=12, re-labeled by a quantum explicit model with the Havlicek et al. encoding plus a hardware-efficient ansatz; M=1000 train, 100 test, 10 labelings), simulated in TensorFlow Quantum, implicit models drive training loss to ~0 at all sizes but generalize poorly. From n=7 qubits onward a clear *test-loss* separation emerges: tuned classical baselines (NN, linear/RBF kernels, random forest, gradient boosting, AdaBoost) become competitive with the quantum kernel models, but explicit variational quantum models clearly outperform both — showing that quantum advantage must not be assessed against kernel methods alone.

**Critical assessment for the hypothesis.** The paper is highly relevant but its evidence is nuanced. (1) The *rigorous* separations are between quantum model classes, and the parity task is explicitly classically easy (learnable by a depth-2 neural net), so it is **not** a proven quantum-over-classical advantage. (2) The genuine quantum-vs-classical signal is the **empirical** fashion-MNIST gap on a fully simulable instance (<=12 qubits; no >=20-qubit or hardware experiments), but it is on a *deliberately quantum-relabeled* task whose encoding is only *conjectured* (not proven) classically intractable, the advantage is in test MSE rather than a complexity-theoretic hardness result, and no dequantization of the explicit-model advantage is given (nor is one ruled out). (3) The authors flag trainability caveats — barren plateaus for explicit models and exponential kernel concentration for implicit models — both of which can demand exponentially many measurements. Net: strong support for "kernel methods are not the right yardstick and variational/data-reuploading models can win," but the demonstrated wins are empirical and task-engineered, and the proven separations sit within the quantum-model family rather than establishing classical intractability. Code: https://github.com/sjerbi/QML-beyond-kernel.

title: The effect of data encoding on the expressive power of variational quantum machine learning models
authors: Maria Schuld, Ryan Sweke, Johannes Jakob Meyer
one_line: Shows that variational quantum models with Hamiltonian time-evolution data encoding can be written exactly as partial Fourier series whose accessible frequencies are set by the encoding-gate eigenvalues and whose coefficients are set by the rest of the circuit, yielding an asymptotic universal-function-approximation result.
methodology: The authors define a quantum model as f(x)=<0|U(x,θ)^† M U(x,θ)|0> with L layers alternating data-encoding blocks S(x)=e^{-ixH} and arbitrary trainable unitaries W. By diagonalizing the encoding Hamiltonian, they rewrite f(x) as a sum of basis functions e^{iωx} where the frequency spectrum Ω is the set of eigenvalue differences (Λ_k − Λ_j); for integer eigenvalues this is a (multivariate) partial Fourier series. They derive that repeating a single-qubit Pauli encoding r times (in parallel as L=1 tensor products, or in series as L=r layers) extends the spectrum to a degree-r truncated Fourier series, give an upper bound K ≤ d^{2L}/2 − 1 on spectrum size, and prove that with arbitrarily-flexible trainable blocks and a universal Hamiltonian family the model becomes an asymptotically universal L2 function approximator.
datasets: No real-world datasets. Purely synthetic target functions used in PennyLane simulations: degree-1 and degree-2 truncated Fourier series g(x)=Σ c_n e^{-nix} with c_0=0.1, c_1=c_2=0.15−0.15i (Fig. 3), and a degree-5 series g(x)=Σ_{n=-5}^{5} c_n e^{2inx} with c_n=0.05−0.05i, c_0=0 (Fig. 4). Sample/batch size 25, up to 200 Adam steps (lr 0.3).
baselines: No classical ML baselines and no competing quantum-model baselines in the quantitative sense. The conceptual reference point is classical Fourier analysis (the model is compared against the target truncated Fourier series it is meant to fit). Theoretical relations are drawn to prior QML expressivity/universality work (Pérez-Salinas et al. data re-uploading, Killoran et al. photonic NN emulation, Gil Vidal & Theis Ref. [28]).
metrics: Mean squared error between sampled target data and the quantum model output during training; qualitative fit quality (degree of Fourier series fit); spectrum size K and degree D as expressivity measures; number of trainable degrees of freedom M ≥ 2K+1 needed to control coefficients.
key_results: A single Pauli-rotation encoding (L=1) can only represent f(x)=A sin(2γx+B)+C, i.e. a single-frequency (degree-1) Fourier series, regardless of circuit width/depth or number of qubits. Repeating the encoding r times (parallel or sequential) gives exactly a degree-r truncated Fourier series; Ω_par = Ω_seq = {−r,...,0,...,r}. Upper bound on spectrum size K ≤ d^{2L}/2 − 1, tightening to K=L for repeated single-qubit encodings. Need M ≥ 2K+1 trainable parameters to realize arbitrary coefficients. Empirically (Fig. 4), r=5 repetitions fit a degree-5 target almost perfectly; r=1,3 underfit. With input rescaling x→2x a frequency mismatch makes a degree-1 model unable to fit (Fig. 3). Universality theorem: for any g∈L2([0,2π]^N) and ε>0, a multivariate L=1 model with a universal Hamiltonian family achieves ||f_{m'}−g||_2 ≤ ε as the number of subsystems m→∞ (with m=K for tensor-product Pauli encodings, or m=O(log K) with global Hamiltonians).
qubits: No fixed/large qubit count; this is a theory paper. Demonstrations use a single qubit (Fig. 3) or r qubits for parallel Pauli encodings with r ∈ {1,3,5} (Fig. 4). Universality is an asymptotic statement requiring the number of subsystems m→∞ (m=K subsystems to reach degree K). No experiments at or above 20 qubits.
classical_struggle: No. The paper does NOT demonstrate any task where classical ML fails and quantum wins. Its central message is the opposite of a quantum-advantage claim: the learnable function class of these quantum models is exactly a (truncated) partial Fourier series, an entirely classical and—at the demonstrated scales—classically simulable/dequantizable object. The authors stress that expressivity is fundamentally capped by the data-encoding strategy even for circuits "which may even be classically intractable to simulate," and they explicitly warn that fair comparisons to classical models must feed the same classically pre-processed features to the classical model (Sec. IV A), implicitly cautioning against spurious advantage claims. The only universality result is (a) asymptotic (requires Hilbert-space dimension / subsystem count → ∞), (b) conditional on trainable blocks being arbitrary global unitaries that may need exponential depth, and (c) merely matches the classical universal-approximation property of Fourier series rather than exceeding any classical method. Whether such expressivity holds for constant/log/poly-depth trainable blocks is left as an open question. So relative to the hypothesis: the paper provides expressivity foundations but offers no evidence of classical-hard / quantum-easy separation, and indeed leans toward dequantization.
code: Experiments performed with the PennyLane software library (Ref. [36]); no dedicated repository link given in the read chunks. Framework: PennyLane.
relevance: High relevance as a foundational/theory reference, low as direct evidence. It is a canonical paper for understanding what function classes 20+ qubit simulable variational models can express (partial Fourier series), which is essential for designing tasks and reasoning about expressivity/frequency matching. But it provides no empirical or proven classical-vs-quantum advantage, and its framing actually cautions against naive advantage claims, so it does not itself support the hypothesis that quantum wins where classical struggles.

#### schuld2021_data_encoding_fourier

**Schuld, Sweke, and Meyer (2021), "The effect of data encoding on the expressive power of variational quantum machine learning models."** This foundational theory paper establishes the now-canonical result that a variational quantum model f(x)=⟨0|U(x,θ)^† M U(x,θ)|0⟩, built from layers of data-encoding blocks S(x)=e^{−ixH} interleaved with arbitrary trainable unitaries W, can be written *exactly* as a partial Fourier series f(x)=Σ_{ω∈Ω} c_ω e^{iωx}. Crucially, the accessible frequency spectrum Ω={Λ_k−Λ_j} is determined *solely* by the eigenvalues of the data-encoding Hamiltonian, while the Fourier coefficients c_ω are controlled by the rest of the circuit and the observable.

**Key technical results.** (1) A single Pauli-rotation encoding (L=1) can only realize f(x)=A sin(2γx+B)+C — a degree-1 Fourier series — *irrespective of the number of qubits, the trainable unitaries, or the measurement*. (2) Repeating the encoding r times, either in parallel (L=1 tensor product) or sequentially (L=r layers), extends the model to a degree-r truncated Fourier series, with Ω_par=Ω_seq={−r,…,0,…,r}. (3) The spectrum size is bounded by K ≤ d^{2L}/2 − 1 (tightening to K=L for repeated single-qubit encodings), and arbitrary control of the K+1 coefficients requires at least M ≥ 2K+1 trainable parameters. (4) An asymptotic *universality theorem*: for any g∈L²([0,2π]^N) and ε>0, a multivariate L=1 model with a "universal Hamiltonian family" can achieve ‖f_{m'}−g‖₂ ≤ ε as the number of subsystems m→∞ — but only under the strong assumption that trainable blocks realize arbitrary global unitaries (potentially exponential depth).

**Empirical illustration.** All experiments are PennyLane simulations on synthetic Fourier targets at very small scale (1 qubit in Fig. 3; r∈{1,3,5} qubits in Fig. 4). They confirm the theory: a degree-1 model fits a degree-1 target but fails after an x→2x rescaling (frequency mismatch) and cannot fit a degree-2 target; r=5 repetitions fit a degree-5 target almost perfectly. There are no real-world datasets, no classical ML baselines, and no experiments approaching 20 qubits.

**Relevance to the hypothesis.** This paper is a high-value *foundational reference* for reasoning about what simulable variational models can express, but it provides **no evidence of a classical-hard / quantum-easy separation**. On the contrary, its core finding is that the learnable function class is exactly a classical Fourier series — a classically tractable, effectively dequantized object at the demonstrated scales. The universality result only *matches* the classical universal-approximation property of Fourier series, is asymptotic (Hilbert-space dimension → ∞), and is conditional on possibly-exponential-depth trainable blocks (the constant/log/poly-depth case is left open). The authors explicitly caution that comparisons to classical ML must use the *same* pre-processed features, implicitly warning against spurious advantage claims. In short: essential for understanding expressivity and frequency matching when designing 20+ qubit tasks, but it does not itself demonstrate quantum advantage.

title: The Expressive Power of Parameterized Quantum Circuits
authors: Yuxuan Du, Min-Hsiu Hsieh, Tongliang Liu, Dacheng Tao
one_line: Proves that parameterized quantum circuits (MPQCs) have provably stronger expressive power than classical generative neural networks (DBM, RBM) and tensor-network PQCs for generative modeling, unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses, and introduces a post-selection-based Bayesian Quantum Circuit (BQC) for learning prior distributions.
methodology: The authors map MPQCs and tensor-network PQCs (TPQCs) onto tensor-network states (MPS/SBS) and use entanglement entropy (area vs. volume law) as a proxy for expressive power, establishing the ordering MPQC > DBM > long-range RBM > TPQC > short-range RBM. They then prove (Theorem 7) that any IQP circuit with N qubits and poly(N) commuting gates can be exactly simulated by an MPQC with O(N) blocks (each <= 7N single-qubit gates and N-1 CNOTs); combined with the Bremner-Jozsa-Shepherd hardness of classically sampling IQP, this gives Theorem 6: MPQCs generate distributions no classical neural network can efficiently sample unless PH collapses. They further introduce ancillary-driven MPQCs (AD-MPQCs) and a special case, the Bayesian Quantum Circuit (BQC), using ancillary qubits with post-selection to represent/learn prior, likelihood and posterior distributions, proving BQC equals post-IQP in power.
datasets: Synthetic/engineered only. (1) Bars-and-Stripes (BAS) images: 2x2 (N_BAS=6 valid images, 4 data qubits) and 3x3 (N_BAS=14, 9 data qubits). (2) A toy prior-learning task: data sampled from a 2-class mixture p(x,lambda)=p(lambda)p(x|lambda) with two Gaussians N1(mu=16,sigma=2), N2(mu=64,sigma=4) encoded into 7 qubits; target class priors p(lambda1)=0.70 and 0.85. No real-world datasets.
baselines: Classical generative baselines treated theoretically: restricted Boltzmann machines (short- and long-range RBM) and deep Boltzmann machines (DBM), characterized via tensor-network/entanglement equivalences. Empirical baselines (BAS task): two prior PQC generative models, DDQCL (data-driven quantum circuit learning, Benedetti et al.) and QCBM (quantum circuit Born machine, Liu and Wang). The complexity-theoretic hardness baseline is classical sampling of IQP distributions.
metrics: Theory: entanglement entropy (area vs. volume law), bond dimension, gate/block counts, polynomial-hierarchy collapse argument. Experiments: BAS generation accuracy (= N_BAS/N, fraction of generated images that are valid BAS patterns); MMD loss convergence; learned prior probabilities P(lambda1), P(lambda2) and their variance vs. target.
key_results: BAS accuracy (Table I): 2x2 — DDQCL 83.82%, QCBM 98.46%, BQC 99.96%; 3x3 — QCBM 65.36%, BQC 98.65% (DDQCL not reported). BQC used N=4 data + M=3 ancilla qubits, L=2 blocks, 48 trainable params (2x2); N=9 + M=4, 112 params (3x3). Prior learning (Table II, M=1, N=7, unbiased gradient estimator): target P(lambda1)=0.70 -> 0.706 (100 shots, var 1.66e-4), 0.702 (1000), 0.701 (inf, var 6.12e-10); target 0.85 -> 0.868/0.856/0.855. A naive ('QVM*') optimizer failed badly (0.163 for 0.70 target). Core theoretical result: provable ordering MPQC > DBM > long-range RBM > TPQC > short-range RBM, and MPQC distributions are classically hard unless PH collapses.
qubits: All experiments are small simulations on Rigetti's pyQuil Quantum Virtual Machine (QVM): BAS 2x2 uses 7 qubits (4 data + 3 ancilla), BAS 3x3 uses 13 qubits (9 data + 4 ancilla), prior-learning uses 8 qubits (7 data + 1 ancilla). No hardware runs. No experiment reaches 20 qubits; the >=20-qubit regime is addressed only by the asymptotic poly(N) theory, not empirically.
classical_struggle: Partially, and mostly theoretically. The strongest claim (Theorems 6-7) is a genuine separation: MPQCs can produce probability distributions that NO classical neural network (incl. DBM / long-range RBM) can sample efficiently and accurately unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses — a complexity-theoretic (conjectured-but-standard) hardness inherited from the IQP / Bremner-Jozsa-Shepherd result, not a learning/accuracy advantage on a concrete dataset. Important caveats: (1) The separation is about sampling expressivity, not about learning or generalization, and gives no efficient training guarantee. (2) The IQP-style hard instances are NOT in the <=30-qubit simulable regime in any demonstrated way — hardness is asymptotic; small instances (the 7-13 qubit BAS/prior tasks actually run) are trivially classically simulable, and on those the 'baselines' beaten are other quantum circuits (QCBM/DDQCL), not classical generative models. (3) The empirical BQC advantage (99.96% vs 98.46% BAS accuracy) is over other PQCs, so it does not evidence classical-vs-quantum separation. (4) Entanglement-entropy 'expressive power' is a representational proxy and the area/volume-law comparison is a known, somewhat coarse argument. (5) No dequantization is discussed, but the IQP hardness does not preclude classical approximate sampling for the specific easy tasks tested. Net: a proven expressivity separation in principle, but no demonstrated task where a 20+-qubit simulable quantum method empirically beats a trained classical model.
code: No public repository. Experiments implemented in Python via pyQuil on Rigetti Forest QVM; paper states "Python codes for our experiments are available upon request." Framework: pyQuil / Rigetti Forest.
relevance: Medium-high. Directly on-topic for the hypothesis as a foundational theoretical pillar: it gives one of the cleanest arguments that PQC generative models are strictly more expressive than classical neural generative models (RBM/DBM) under PH-non-collapse. However, relevance to the SPECIFIC hypothesis (simulable 20+-qubit quantum methods empirically beating classical ML) is limited because all separations are asymptotic/complexity-theoretic, all experiments are <=13 qubits, and empirical comparisons are quantum-vs-quantum rather than quantum-vs-classical. Useful for motivation and theory framing, not as direct evidence of a simulable empirical advantage.

#### du2020_expressive_power_pqc

**Du, Hsieh, Liu & Tao (2018/2020), "The Expressive Power of Parameterized Quantum Circuits."**

This paper provides a foundational theoretical comparison of the expressive power of parameterized quantum circuits (PQCs) against classical generative neural networks for generative modeling. The authors map two PQC families — multilayer PQCs (MPQCs, with arbitrary CNOT connectivity) and tensor-network PQCs (TPQCs, with local connectivity) — onto tensor-network states (matrix product states and string-bond states), and use entanglement entropy (area- vs. volume-law scaling) as a proxy for representational capacity. Their central result (Theorem 3) establishes a strict ordering, **MPQC > DBM > long-range RBM > TPQC > short-range RBM**, given O(poly(N)) parameters. The headline separation (Theorems 6–7) is complexity-theoretic: any IQP circuit on N qubits with poly(N) commuting gates can be exactly compiled into an MPQC of O(N) blocks (each with <= 7N single-qubit gates and N-1 CNOTs), so by the Bremner–Jozsa–Shepherd hardness of classically sampling IQP, **MPQCs generate distributions that no classical neural network can sample efficiently and accurately unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses.** The paper additionally introduces ancillary-driven MPQCs and a Bayesian Quantum Circuit (BQC) — a post-selection construction (equivalent in power to post-IQP) that can explicitly learn prior, likelihood, and posterior distributions, which the authors highlight as the first PQC able to learn prior distributions from data for semi-supervised settings.

Empirically, the work is small-scale and runs only in simulation on Rigetti's pyQuil Quantum Virtual Machine (no hardware). On the Bars-and-Stripes (BAS) generative benchmark, BQC reaches 99.96% accuracy on 2x2 images (7 qubits: 4 data + 3 ancilla, 48 params) and 98.65% on 3x3 images (13 qubits: 9 data + 4 ancilla, 112 params), versus QCBM (98.46% / 65.36%) and DDQCL (83.82% / —). A toy 8-qubit (7 data + 1 ancilla) prior-learning task recovers target class priors accurately when using an unbiased gradient estimator (e.g., learning 0.701 vs. target 0.70 at infinite shots, variance 6.12e-10), while a naive optimizer fails.

**Relevance to the hypothesis (medium–high).** The paper supplies one of the cleanest *theoretical* separations between quantum and classical generative models, making it a strong motivational reference. However, it offers no direct support for the specific claim that *simulable, 20+-qubit* quantum methods empirically outperform classical ML: (i) the separation is asymptotic and complexity-theoretic (sampling expressivity, not learning/generalization), and is conditional on PH non-collapse; (ii) the provably-hard IQP instances are not demonstrated in the <=30-qubit simulable regime, and the instances actually run (7–13 qubits) are trivially classically simulable; (iii) all empirical comparisons pit BQC against *other quantum circuits* (QCBM, DDQCL), not against trained classical generative models, so the 99.96% vs. 98.46% gap is not classical-vs-quantum evidence; and (iv) the entanglement-entropy argument is a coarse representational proxy with no efficient-trainability guarantee. In short, a proven in-principle expressivity advantage, but not an empirical demonstration of a simulable quantum win over classical baselines.

title: Enhancing Generative Models via Quantum Correlations
authors: Xun Gao, Eric R. Anschuetz, Sheng-Tao Wang, J. Ignacio Cirac, Mikhail D. Lukin
one_line: Proves that quantum correlations (nonlocality and contextuality) give a minimal "basis-enhanced" quantum extension of Bayesian networks unconditionally greater expressive power than classical Bayesian networks/HMMs, and demonstrates the gap numerically on real ML datasets via classically-simulable tensor networks.
methodology: The authors define Bayesian Quantum Circuits (BQCs), which reproduce classical Bayesian-network distributions, and a minimal extension, Basis-enhanced BQCs (BBQCs), that only adds arbitrary local (single-qubit) measurement bases; BBQCs are special tensor networks (MPS/MPO). They prove two unconditional expressivity separations: (1) a basis-enhanced 2-gram model (cluster state with X/Y measurements embedding a GHZ state) cannot be approximated under finite-KL error by any classical k-gram model with k=o(n), via quantum nonlocality (Theorem 1); (2) a basis-enhanced HMM with state space D needs D^Omega(log D) hidden units to approximate classically, via quantum contextuality and the Mermin–Peres magic square (Theorem 2). They also recap a complexity-theory-based exponential separation for 2D models (Theorem 3, multiplicative-error, #P-hard, would collapse the polynomial hierarchy). Empirically they train basis-enhanced 2-gram models vs classical HMMs by Riemannian (unitary) gradient descent and compare normalized KL divergence over bond dimensions.
datasets: Three real ML sequential datasets: biofam (family life-course sequences; length n=16, output alphabet M=8) used as a "local" control; Promoter Gene Sequences (DNA promoter vs non-promoter; n=57, M=4); SPECT Heart (binary feature vectors of heart images; n=23, M=2). A quarter of biofam/Promoter data was held out for testing; the standard SPECT test split was used. No image/NLP large-scale corpora; sizes are small UCI-style sequence datasets.
baselines: Classical baseline: hidden Markov model (HMM, the most generic 1D sequential generative model, subsuming k-gram models and finite-precision feedforward/recurrent nets). Quantum-enhanced model: basis-enhanced 2-gram model (a special MPS/MPO). Both swept over bond dimension h = 2 to 12; a likelihood-ratio test controls for the extra parameters of the quantum model.
metrics: Stochastic estimate of forward KL divergence (in bits, normalized by sequence length) on training and held-out test data vs bond dimension; best-of-ten-trials and average-over-runs; likelihood-ratio test for statistical significance of the KL improvement (5σ threshold).
key_results: Basis-enhanced 2-gram model beats classical HMM on both train and test KL for Promoter Gene Sequences and SPECT Heart, and ties on the local-structure control biofam. The KL improvement reaches roughly up to ~2.5 bits (Promoter) and ~1 bit (SPECT) at bond dimension h=12, and is statistically significant at >5σ via the likelihood-ratio test (rejecting the null that the quantum model collapses to a classical HMM). Theoretical separations: 2 vs O(n) in k for k-gram models (nonlocality); D vs D^Omega(log D) hidden units for HMMs (contextuality); an HMM of bond dimension 6 can reproduce the basis-enhanced 2-gram construction. Separation persists under average (not just best) performance.
qubits: No large hardware run and no explicit >=20-qubit simulation count is reported. Constructions are 1D and classically simulable: the basis-enhanced 2-gram is a cluster/GHZ state where each variable = 2 qubits (so the n=16/23/57-variable datasets correspond to roughly 2n ~ 32/46/114 logical qubits worth of cluster state, but evaluated as MPS with bond dimension <=12, not as a qubit simulator). The genuinely quantum-hardware-requiring example (Theorem 3) is a 2D cluster state on a square lattice with n qubits; no specific qubit number is given and it was not simulated.
classical_struggle: Partially, with important caveats. The paper gives UNCONDITIONAL proofs that classical k-gram and HMM models provably cannot match the basis-enhanced (quantum-correlation-inspired) models under a finite-KL error model — a real "classical struggle" result rooted in nonlocality/contextuality. However, the winning models in the proofs and ALL numerical experiments are 1D tensor networks (MPS/MPO) that are EFFICIENTLY CLASSICALLY SIMULABLE; the authors explicitly frame these as quantum-inspired CLASSICAL algorithms, so this is not a case of simulable-quantum beating all-classical — a classical computer runs both sides. The only example needing a real quantum computer is the 2D-Bayesian-network/cluster-state construction (Theorem 3), whose separation is CONDITIONAL (assumes the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse, multiplicative-error model) and is NOT demonstrated empirically. The finite-KL error model is also idealized (relies on exact-zero probability structure), and the empirical gap is modest (1–2.5 bits) and uses extra parameters (mitigated by the 5σ likelihood-ratio test). So: provable classical limitation in expressivity yes; a simulated >=20-qubit quantum model beating classical on a practical task with proven advantage — not shown.
code: None explicitly linked in the read sections; custom Riemannian/unitary gradient-descent training of MPS/MPO tensor networks (method adapted from their Ref. [61]). No Qiskit/PennyLane/TFQ repo mentioned.
relevance: Medium. Highly relevant conceptually: it is one of the cleanest results identifying WHY quantum models can outdo classical generative models (nonlocality/contextuality) and shows an empirical expressivity gap on real datasets. But it is medium rather than high for the specific hypothesis because the demonstrated advantage uses classically-simulable 1D tensor networks (quantum-inspired classical methods), not 20+ qubit quantum simulation; the truly quantum-only advantage is 2D, conditional, and untested. Useful as a theoretical anchor and a cautionary example that many "quantum advantage" generative results dequantize to classical MPS.
classical_struggle_short: unconditional expressivity separation proven; but winning models are classically-simulable MPS (quantum-inspired), and the quantum-hardware-only 2D case is conditional and untested

#### gao2022_quantum_generative

**Gao, Anschuetz, Wang, Cirac, Lukin (2022) — "Enhancing Generative Models via Quantum Correlations"** (arXiv:2101.08354; Phys. Rev. X).

*Contribution.* This paper isolates the physical origin of quantum advantage in generative modeling by introducing a *minimal* quantum extension of classical Bayesian networks. Starting from Bayesian Quantum Circuits (BQCs), which exactly reproduce a Bayesian network's distribution under computational-basis measurement, they define **Basis-enhanced BQCs (BBQCs)**, which change only the local measurement basis. BBQCs remain special tensor networks (MPS/MPO), so the quantum "ingredient" is added with minimal overhead and the source of any advantage can be pinpointed.

*Theory.* They prove two **unconditional** expressivity separations under a finite forward/reverse KL-divergence error model: (1) **Nonlocality (Theorem 1):** a basis-enhanced 2-gram model (a cluster state measured in X/Y embedding a GHZ state) cannot be approximated by any classical *k*-gram model with k = o(n), giving a 2-vs-O(n) separation. (2) **Contextuality (Theorem 2):** a basis-enhanced HMM with state space D requires D^Ω(log D) classical hidden units, proven via the Mermin–Peres magic square and the impossibility of common hidden variables across a contextual triple of stabilizer states. They also restate a **conditional** complexity result (Theorem 3): for 2D Bayesian networks (square-lattice cluster states), no polynomial-size classical Bayesian network can reproduce the distribution to multiplicative error unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses (#P ⊆ Σ₂ᵖ/poly).

*Experiments.* Because the 1D models are classically simulable, both classical HMMs and basis-enhanced 2-gram models are trained directly (via a unitary/Riemannian gradient-descent variant) on three real datasets: **biofam** (n=16, M=8; local-structure control), **Promoter Gene Sequences** (n=57, M=4), and **SPECT Heart** (n=23, M=2), sweeping bond dimension h = 2–12. The basis-enhanced model achieves lower train and test KL on Promoter and SPECT (improvements up to roughly 2.5 and 1 bits, respectively, at h=12) and ties on biofam as expected. A likelihood-ratio test, which accounts for the quantum model's extra parameters, rejects the "collapses-to-classical-HMM" null at **>5σ** on both non-local datasets, and the gap persists in average (not just best-of-ten) performance.

*Critical assessment for the hypothesis.* This is a strong, rare example of an **unconditional, mechanistically-explained** expressivity gap between classical and quantum-correlation-enhanced generative models, validated on real data. However, several caveats temper its support for the specific hypothesis of "simulable (≤30-qubit) quantum beats classical": (i) every empirical result and both unconditional theorems use **1D tensor networks (MPS/MPO) that are efficiently classically simulable** — the authors themselves frame these as *quantum-inspired classical* algorithms, so a classical computer runs both the "quantum" and classical sides; this is effectively a dequantized advantage. (ii) The only construction that genuinely needs a quantum computer (2D cluster states) is **conditional** (polynomial-hierarchy, multiplicative-error) and is **not simulated or benchmarked**. (iii) No ≥20-qubit simulator or hardware experiment is reported; qubit counts are implicit (each variable ≈ 2 qubits) and the models are evaluated as bond-dimension-≤12 MPS rather than statevector. (iv) The finite-KL error model is idealized and the measured empirical gap, while significant, is modest (1–2.5 bits). **Relevance: medium** — valuable as theoretical grounding for *why* quantum correlations help and as a cautionary datapoint that many generative "quantum advantages" reduce to classical tensor networks; less directly a demonstration of large-qubit simulated quantum dominance.

title: Quantum embeddings for machine learning
authors: Seth Lloyd, Maria Schuld, Aroosa Ijaz, Josh Izaac, Nathan Killoran
one_line: Proposes "quantum metric learning": instead of training a variational measurement, train the quantum feature map (embedding) to maximally separate data classes in Hilbert space, after which the optimal classifying measurement is known in closed form (Helstrom for trace/l1 distance, fidelity/overlap for Hilbert-Schmidt/l2 distance).
methodology: Classical data are embedded as quantum states via a trainable QAOA-inspired feature map (layers of input-encoding Rx rotations, trainable ZZ Ising entanglers, and Ry rotations), with optional "latent" qubits to raise Hilbert-space dimension. Rather than training a post-embedding variational circuit, the embedding parameters theta are optimized (via PennyLane + classical gradient descent / RMSProp / Adagrad) to maximize the Hilbert-Schmidt (or trace) distance between the class density matrices rho and sigma; the cost is C = 1 - (1/2)D_hs, estimated on a quantum computer using SWAP tests or "inversion tests". The optimal measurement is then analytically determined: a fidelity/overlap classifier minimizes risk for the l2 metric, and a Helstrom (positive/negative eigenspace projection of rho-sigma) classifier for the l1/trace metric. Low-rank embeddings emerge naturally and aid generalization and sample complexity.
datasets: Toy/illustrative datasets only: (1) 2-D "moons" dataset (Fig. 2); (2) a synthetic non-overlapping but not linearly separable 1-D dataset embedded into a single qubit (Fig. 4); (3) an "ants vs bees" image classification task in a hybrid pipeline where images pass through a pre-trained ResNet whose 512-dim output is reduced by a trainable linear layer to a 2-D feature fed into the quantum circuit (Fig. 5). No dataset sizes/counts reported; batch sizes of 2-5 used.
baselines: No quantitative comparison to classical ML baselines. Conceptual baseline is prior quantum work (Schuld-Killoran 2019; Havlicek et al. 2019) that fixes the embedding and trains the measurement; this paper inverts that. The two proposed quantum classifiers (Helstrom vs fidelity) are compared to each other. Classical "deep metric learning" (Siamese/contrastive nets) is cited as the conceptual analog but not benchmarked.
metrics: Training cost C = 1 - (1/2)D_hs (range [0,2]); empirical risk = -D_hs (fidelity classifier) or -2 D_tr (Helstrom classifier); inter-cluster overlap tr(rho sigma) and purities tr(rho^2), tr(sigma^2); decision-boundary visualizations; Bloch-sphere / Hilbert-space mutual-overlap heatmaps.
key_results: Demonstrations are qualitative. Moons dataset: 2-qubit QAOA feature map, 4 layers, 500 RMSProp steps, batch size 5, final cost 0.28 for l1 and 0.55 for l2 training; Helstrom and fidelity classifiers give similar (not identical) boundaries, Helstrom outputs closer to extremes +/-1. 1-D toy: single qubit, 200 steps, learning rate 0.01, batch size 2, classes become well-separated and linearly separable on the Bloch sphere. Hybrid ResNet+quantum: after 1500 Adagrad steps (batch 2), classes "perfectly" separated in quantum feature space (overlap heatmap goes block-diagonal). No accuracy/error numbers, no classical comparison.
qubits: Very small in experiments: 1-qubit and 2-qubit embeddings (simulated analytically in PennyLane). No experiment uses >=20 qubits. Larger numbers appear only in feasibility/illustrative discussion: a 2^50 ~ 10^15 dim embedding needing 50 controlled-SWAPs on 101 qubits; near-term hardware estimates cite ~100 superconducting or ion-trap qubits able to embed O(10^10)-O(10^11) classical bits within coherence time. SWAP test needs 2n+1 qubits; inversion test needs only n qubits.
classical_struggle: The paper does NOT demonstrate a task where classical methods fail and quantum wins. The authors explicitly state they "do not attempt to answer the question of whether quantum embeddings are better than classical machine learning methods" and that "the existence of a useful quantum advantage is an open question." The quantum-advantage argument is purely conjectural/complexity-theoretic: deep QAOA/IQP-style embeddings are argued to be classically intractable to reproduce "unless BQP=P", so the class of embeddings is strictly larger than classical ones. But (a) this is a statement about hardness of simulating the embedding, not a proven learning/generalization advantage; (b) it provides no task where classical ML underperforms; (c) the actual experiments use only 1-2 qubits, are trivially classically simulable (done analytically in PennyLane), and the hardest task (ants/bees) leans heavily on a classical ResNet doing most of the feature extraction. No dequantization is discussed, but the claimed intractability applies to >=~50-qubit, depth-O(n) circuits—well beyond the simulable 20-30 qubit regime of interest and beyond anything tested here. Relevance to the hypothesis is therefore conceptual scaffolding (a principled, resource-light framework) rather than evidence.
code: No dedicated repo released; experiments implemented in the PennyLane hybrid-optimization framework (Bergholm et al., arXiv:1811.04968). Classifiers simulated analytically.
relevance: Medium. Highly relevant conceptually—it gives a clean, analytically grounded framework (metric learning in Hilbert space with known-optimal measurements) that is well suited to simulation and near-term devices, and motivates why quantum embeddings could access classifications classical models cannot. But it provides no empirical quantum-advantage evidence, no classical baselines, and uses only 1-2 qubits, so it does not directly support a "classical struggles, simulable quantum wins" claim at 20+ qubits.

#### lloyd2020_quantum_embeddings

**Lloyd, Schuld, Ijaz, Izaac & Killoran (2020), "Quantum embeddings for machine learning" (arXiv:2001.03622).**

This work reframes variational quantum classification as a *quantum metric-learning* problem. Standard variational quantum classifiers fix a data-encoding feature map and train a measurement (a variational circuit followed by a Pauli-Z readout). Lloyd et al. argue this is backwards: since the post-embedding unitary is merely a linear change of measurement basis, almost all of the nonlinear discriminating power lives in the *embedding*. They therefore propose training the feature map itself to **maximally separate the class density matrices rho and sigma in Hilbert space**, after which the optimal classifying measurement is known in closed form—the Helstrom (minimum-error) projection for the trace/l1 distance, and a simple fidelity/overlap measurement for the Hilbert-Schmidt/l2 distance. This eliminates the expensive trainable measurement, a meaningful saving for near-term hardware. Concretely, the linear-loss empirical risk equals -D_hs for the fidelity classifier and -2 D_tr for the Helstrom classifier, so maximizing the respective Hilbert-space distance directly minimizes risk; the training cost C = 1 - (1/2)D_hs is estimable via SWAP or inversion tests. The l2/Hilbert-Schmidt route is favored because it needs far shallower circuits and naturally yields low-rank, tight clusters that aid generalization and reduce sample complexity.

**Method and experiments.** Embeddings use a QAOA-inspired ansatz (input-encoding Rx rotations, trainable ZZ Ising entanglers, Ry "local fields", optional latent qubits), trained in PennyLane with classical gradient descent. Demonstrations are illustrative and tiny: a 2-qubit, 4-layer map on the 2-D "moons" dataset (500 RMSProp steps, final cost 0.28 for l1 / 0.55 for l2); a single-qubit map on a synthetic non-linearly-separable 1-D set (200 steps) that becomes linearly separable on the Bloch sphere; and a hybrid ResNet-to-2-D-to-quantum pipeline on "ants vs bees" images that, after 1500 Adagrad steps, "perfectly" separates the classes in quantum feature space. No accuracy figures, dataset sizes, or classical baselines are reported, and all experiments are at 1-2 qubits and analytically simulated.

**Assessment for the hypothesis.** The paper offers a principled, resource-efficient framework that is attractive for simulation studies, but it provides **no evidence of a task where classical ML fails and quantum wins**. The authors explicitly decline to claim advantage, calling "the existence of a useful quantum advantage... an open question." The advantage argument is purely complexity-theoretic—deep QAOA/IQP-style embeddings are conjectured classically intractable "unless BQP=P"—and concerns the hardness of *simulating the embedding*, not a demonstrated learning or generalization gain. That intractability claim moreover targets ~50+ qubit, depth-O(n) circuits, outside the 20-30 qubit simulable regime of interest and far beyond the 1-2 qubit experiments actually run. Its value here is as conceptual scaffolding (known-optimal measurements, distance-maximizing embeddings, maximum-mean-discrepancy connection) rather than as empirical support. **Relevance: medium.**

title: Shadows of quantum machine learning
authors: Sofiene Jerbi, Casper Gyurik, Simon C. Marshall, Riccardo Molteni, Vedran Dunjko
one_line: Introduces "shadow models" — quantum ML models trained on a quantum computer but deployed entirely classically via shadow tomography of a "flipped" linear model — and proves they retain a provable learning advantage over fully classical learners (under cryptographic assumptions) while being strictly weaker than fully quantum models.
methodology: The authors reformulate all standard QML models as quantum linear models f_theta(x)=Tr[rho(x)O(theta)], then define a "flipped model" f_theta(x)=Tr[rho(theta)O(x)] that swaps the roles of the parametrized state and the data-encoding observable, making rho(theta) a quantum state amenable to classical-shadow tomography. The "shadowing phase" uses a quantum computer to collect a classical description (advice omega(theta)) of rho(theta); the "deployment phase" then evaluates the model on new data x purely classically. They give a general complexity-theoretic definition (class BPP/qgenpoly), prove flipped models are shadow-universal (Lemma 4), prove a quantum advantage via a discrete-cube-root learning task (Theorem 1), and prove not all quantum models are shadowfiable under BQP not-subset-of P/poly (Theorem 5).
datasets: None. The paper explicitly states "Data sharing is not applicable... as no datasets were generated or analyzed." Tasks are abstract/constructed: a discrete-cube-root learning task over Z_N (target g_s(x) keyed to RSA-like trapdoor) used as a theoretical separation, and a database-search toy model on x in {0,pi}^n used to separate flipped-shadowing from Fourier-shadowing.
baselines: Conceptual baselines, not empirical: (i) fully classical learners (class BPP) — separated from shadow models under the DCR assumption; (ii) Fourier-based "classical surrogates" of Schreiber et al. 2023 and Landman et al. 2022 — shown to need exponential sample complexity on a task shadow models handle efficiently; (iii) fully quantum models (BQP) — shown to be strictly more powerful than shadow models (BPP/qgenpoly) under BQP not-subset-of P/poly. No numerical comparison.
metrics: Theoretical complexity-theoretic metrics: classical/quantum sample and query complexity, PAC learnability, number of shadow measurements, generalization bounds (training error vs expected error gap), spectral/trace norm of observables, qubit count. No accuracy/F1 numbers reported.
key_results: Theorem 1: a shadow model can achieve arbitrarily good performance on the discrete-cube-root task while any fully classical model "cannot do significantly better than random guessing" (under DCR/RSA hardness). Flipping a conventional n-qubit model with trace-norm ||O||_1 <= alpha yields a flipped model on m=n+1 qubits with spectral norm ||O'||_inf = alpha. Generalization: training set size M ~ Omega-tilde(n||O||_inf^2 / (eps*eta^2)) suffices (linear in n, quadratic in spectral norm). Local-Pauli shadow cost: O-tilde(3^k B^2 eps^-2) measurements. Fourier-shadowing requires Omega(2^n) queries on a database-search task that flipped-shadowing solves with a single computational-basis measurement. Theorem 5: under BQP not-subset-of P/poly, models computing discrete-log are not shadowfiable.
qubits: Theoretical, parameter n (generic n-qubit / m=n+1-qubit constructions); no specific simulated or hardware qubit count is run. No experiments at 20+ qubits (or any qubit count) were performed — the paper is entirely analytical.
classical_struggle: Yes, but only as a PROVABLE-IN-THEORY (conjectural-hardness) separation, not an empirical demonstration. The discrete-cube-root task (Theorem 1) is a genuine learning task where fully classical learners provably cannot beat random guessing while a shadow model succeeds — contingent on the DCR/RSA cryptographic hardness assumption (a widely-believed but unproven complexity conjecture, analogous to Liu-Arunachalam-Temme 2021). Important caveats: (1) The task is admittedly "somewhat contrived" — a cryptographic trapdoor construction, not a physically/practically motivated dataset; the authors flag finding physically-relevant analogues (e.g., ground-state properties) as future work. (2) The advantage relies on a quantum computer during TRAINING/shadowing (Shor's algorithm to find the trapdoor key), so it is NOT a dequantization — deployment is classical but the quantum step is essential. (3) The construction uses computational-basis states, so the "winning" quantum resource is Shor-style number-theoretic computation, not a many-qubit variational simulation; nothing about it requires or demonstrates the 20+-qubit simulation regime in the hypothesis. (4) No numbers, no simulation, no hardware. So the paper supports the hypothesis's existence-claim in principle but provides no simulable >=20-qubit empirical evidence.
code: None. No repository, no Qiskit/PennyLane/TFQ implementation; explicitly no data or code (theoretical paper).
relevance: Medium. Highly relevant conceptually — it rigorously establishes that a quantum-trained, classically-deployed model can solve a task classical ML provably cannot (supporting the "classical struggles, quantum wins" premise) and clarifies the BPP < BPP/qgenpoly < BQP landscape. But low on the specific operational angle of the hypothesis: there are no simulations, no 20+-qubit experiments, no datasets, and the advantage rests on a contrived cryptographic task with a Shor-based (not simulation-based) quantum step. Useful as a theoretical anchor/framing citation rather than an empirical demonstration.
markdown_section:
#### jerbi2023_shadows_qml

**Jerbi, Gyurik, Marshall, Molteni & Dunjko (2023/2024), "Shadows of quantum machine learning."**

This theoretical paper addresses a deployment obstacle in QML: even after training, most quantum models still need a quantum computer at inference time. The authors introduce **shadow models** — models trained (and "shadowed") on a quantum computer but evaluated entirely classically on new data. The key device is to express every standard QML model as a quantum linear model `f_θ(x)=Tr[ρ(x)O(θ)]` and then define a **flipped model** `f_θ(x)=Tr[ρ(θ)O(x)]`, swapping the parametrized state and the data-encoding observable. Because `ρ(θ)` is now a genuine quantum state, classical-shadow tomography produces a classical description (advice `ω(θ)`, independent of future inputs) that a polynomial-time classical algorithm uses at deployment. They prove (Lemma 4) that flipped models are *shadow-universal*: every shadowfiable model is captured by a shadowed flipped model, and they characterize the class as `BPP/qgenpoly` (classical poly-time with quantum-generated poly advice).

**Main claims.** (i) *Quantum advantage (Theorem 1):* on a discrete-cube-root learning task over `Z_N` (an RSA-style trapdoor function), a shadow model — using Shor's algorithm during the quantum shadowing phase to recover the trapdoor key, then a single computational-basis measurement — achieves arbitrarily good accuracy, while **any fully classical learner cannot beat random guessing**, under the discrete-cube-root (DCR) hardness assumption. (ii) *Beyond Fourier:* the alternative "classical surrogate" approach of Schreiber et al. and Landman et al. can require `Ω(2^n)` queries (database-search lower bound) on a task that flipped-shadowing solves with one measurement, showing shadow models strictly generalize Fourier surrogates. (iii) *Not all models are shadowfiable (Theorem 5):* under `BQP ⊄ P/poly`, models computing the discrete logarithm are not shadowfiable, placing shadow models strictly between classical (`BPP`) and fully quantum (`BQP`) models. They also give operational generalization bounds (training size `M ~ Õ(n·‖O‖_∞² / (ε·η²))`, linear in qubit count `n`, quadratic in spectral norm) and shadow-cost bounds (`Õ(3^k B² ε^{-2})` measurements for `k`-local Paulis).

**Assessment for the hypothesis ("classical struggles, quantum wins via ≥20-qubit simulation").** The paper *does* exhibit a task where classical methods provably fail and a quantum-enabled model wins — but with major caveats relative to our specific framing. The separation is **conjectural-hardness-based** (DCR/RSA, akin to Liu–Arunachalam–Temme 2021), **not empirical**: the paper contains **no datasets, no simulations, no hardware, and no qubit counts** beyond a generic parameter `n`, and explicitly states no data were generated. The quantum resource that defeats the classical learner is **Shor's algorithm on computational-basis states**, not a many-qubit *variational simulation*, so the result neither requires nor demonstrates the 20+-qubit simulable regime central to our hypothesis. The authors themselves call the trapdoor task "somewhat contrived" and flag physically-relevant instantiations (e.g., predicting ground-state properties) as future work. **Relevance: medium** — valuable as a rigorous conceptual anchor for the existence of classical-deployment quantum advantage and for the `BPP < BPP/qgenpoly < BQP` taxonomy, but it offers no simulable, numerical, or large-qubit evidence and should be cited for framing rather than as empirical support. *Code/data: none.*

title: A quantum-inspired classical algorithm for recommendation systems
one_line: A classical ("quantum-inspired") sampling algorithm that solves the recommendation-systems task in time polylogarithmic in matrix dimensions, "dequantizing" the Kerenidis-Prakash quantum recommendation algorithm and proving it gives no exponential quantum speedup.
methodology: Replaces quantum state-preparation assumptions with classical l2-norm sampling assumptions supported by the same dynamic data structure (a binary-search-tree structure storing row/entry magnitudes, O(log mn) overhead). The core routine (ModFKV, built on Frieze-Kannan-Vempala 1998) draws l2-norm samples to find a constant-sized description (S, U-hat, Sigma-hat) of a low-rank approximation D = A V-hat V-hat^T, then uses inner-product estimation plus rejection sampling to draw an l2-norm sample from row i of D. This produces samples eps-close in total-variation distance to the quantum algorithm's output distribution, in time independent of m and n.
datasets: None. This is a purely theoretical complexity-theory / algorithms paper with proofs only; no experiments, no real or synthetic datasets, no numerical benchmarks. The data model is an abstract m x n preference matrix T assumed close to rank k, with a subsampled matrix A held in the data structure (recommendation model of Kumar et al. 1998 / Azar et al. 2001 / Drineas et al. 2002).
baselines: Conceptual/analytical comparisons (no empirical runs): Kerenidis-Prakash 2017 quantum recommendation algorithm (the dequantization target), classical matrix-completion methods (linear time in m,n), and prior sublinear sampling-based recommendation approaches (Drineas et al. 2002, Kumar et al. 2001, Azar et al. 2001).
metrics: Asymptotic query/time complexity; total-variation distance (eps-closeness) of output sampling distribution; rank/Frobenius-norm bounds on the low-rank approximation; recommendation-quality bounds (matched to KP17b up to constants).
key_results: Classical algorithm runs in O(poly(k) polylog(m,n)) time, i.e. independent of m and n up to the O(log mn) data-structure overhead; explicit bound O~( ||A||_F^24 / (sigma^24 eps^12 eta^6) * log(mn) * ||A_i||^2/||D_i||^2 ). Only polynomially slower than the quantum algorithm (the quantum version has log(1/eps) vs the classical poly(1/eps) dependence). Theorem 2: recommendation-quality bounds identical to the KP17b quantum algorithm up to constant factors. Conclusion: KP17b gives at most a polynomial, not exponential, speedup.
qubits: None. No quantum circuits are simulated or run; no qubit counts appear. The entire contribution is a classical algorithm. The referenced quantum algorithm (KP17b) is discussed only abstractly.
classical_struggle: Strongly negative evidence for the hypothesis. This is the foundational "dequantization" result: it proves that a task previously believed to be one of the best candidates for an exponential QML speedup (Kerenidis-Prakash recommendation systems) can be solved classically in polylog(m,n) time, only polynomially slower than the quantum algorithm. The advantage is removed by a rigorous proof (Theorems 1 and 2), not merely empirically. Crucial condition/caveat: the classical algorithm requires l2-norm sampling access to the input, which Tang argues is the fair classical analogue of the quantum state-preparation assumption; under that matched assumption there is no exponential gap. Caveats on the classical side: large exponents/constants (from FKV), restrictive model assumptions (knowing a constant fraction of T, data held in a special structure) making it impractical, and poly(1/eps) rather than log(1/eps) error dependence. Net: this paper is direct counter-evidence to claims that quantum methods excel where classical methods struggle on this class of low-rank linear-algebra/recommendation tasks.
code: none (theoretical paper; no implementation, repository, or framework provided).
relevance: high - directly relevant as counter-evidence. It is the seminal dequantization paper showing that a flagship QML "exponential speedup" task is in fact efficiently classically solvable, sharpening any claim that quantum methods excel where classical ones fail and establishing the l2-norm-sampling framework needed to fairly compare QML and classical ML.

#### tang2019_dequantization_recommendation

**A quantum-inspired classical algorithm for recommendation systems** (Ewin Tang, 2019).

This is the foundational "dequantization" result and serves as important counter-evidence to the hypothesis that there exist practically relevant tasks where classical ML struggles but quantum ML excels. Tang targets the Kerenidis-Prakash (KP17b) quantum recommendation system, which at publication was exponentially faster than the best known classical algorithms and was regarded (e.g., in Preskill's survey) as one of the strongest candidates for a provable exponential QML speedup. Tang gives a *classical* algorithm performing the same task — drawing an l2-norm sample from a designated row of a low-rank approximation of the input matrix — in time independent of the matrix dimensions m and n, only *polynomially* slower than the quantum algorithm.

**Method.** The key insight is that the dynamic data structure KP use to satisfy quantum *state-preparation* assumptions equally supports classical *l2-norm sampling* assumptions (with only O(log mn) overhead). l2-norm sampling distributions play the role of quantum superpositions. The algorithm runs ModFKV (a variant of Frieze-Kannan-Vempala 1998) to produce a constant-sized description of a low-rank approximation D = A V-hat V-hat^T, then uses inner-product estimation and rejection sampling to emit a sample eps-close in total-variation distance to the quantum output distribution.

**Results.** Theorem 1 gives runtime O(poly(k) polylog(m,n)), independent of m,n (explicitly O~(||A||_F^24 σ^-24 ε^-12 η^-6 · log(mn) · ||A_i||^2/||D_i||^2)); the only essential gap versus the quantum algorithm is poly(1/ε) vs log(1/ε) error dependence. Theorem 2 shows the resulting recommendation quality matches KP17b up to constant factors. The conclusion is that the quantum recommendation algorithm does **not** give an exponential speedup.

**Evaluation type.** Purely theoretical: proofs only, no experiments, no datasets, no code, and **no qubits** (nothing is simulated or run on hardware). The fairness of the comparison hinges on matching the quantum state-preparation assumption with a classical l2-norm-sampling assumption — a framework Tang proposes for all such comparisons.

**Caveats / relevance.** The classical algorithm has large exponents and constants, restrictive model assumptions (it must know a constant fraction of the full preference matrix, with data pre-loaded into a sampling data structure), and is acknowledged to be impractical to deploy directly. Nonetheless, for the literature review this paper is high-relevance negative evidence: it rigorously demonstrates that a flagship "quantum-advantage" ML task is efficiently classically solvable, and establishes the l2-norm-sampling baseline that any candidate quantum-advantage claim on low-rank linear-algebra / recommendation tasks must now surpass.
